title: Achievements
text:
# Achievements

Source: https://cs2.paradoxwikis.com/Achievements

Achievements are unlocked for players after reaching certain milestones, achieving hidden goals, or unlocking certain items. There are currently 38 unlockable achievements in the base game.

## Base Game Achievements

Name Description

A Little Bit of TLC Have 6,000 citizens treated at medical clinics or hospitals in a single city.

All Smiles Have at least 1,000 citizens and an average happiness rating of 75%.

Calling the Shots Have 5city policiesactive simultaneously.

Cartography Use the editor to make a map.

Colossal Gardener Plant 100 trees or bushes with the landscaping tool in a single city.

Everything the Light Touches Unlock 150 map tiles in a single city.

Executive Decision Assign a policyto a city district.

Four Seasons Experience all four seasons by building a city in a climatewith a snowy winter.

Go Anywhere Have 20 active transport lines. These can be passenger or cargo lines or any mix of the two.

Groundskeeper Build 10 parks in a single city.

Happy to Be of Service Create a city district and assign a city service to that district.

I Made This Use the editor to make an asset of any other type than map.

Key to the City Unlock each building available in the base game.

Making a Mark Build 5signature buildingsin a single city.

title: Asset Creation Guide
text:
# Asset Creation Guide

Source: https://cs2.paradoxwikis.com/Asset_Creation_Guide

Verified by Paradox Interactive
This article has been verified by Paradox Interactive for version 1.5.2 f1 of the game.

## General Guidelines

#### Common Terminology in Cities: Skylines II

Asset
Asset can refer to numerous types of content created in the editor, usually referring to the complete, usable content. Assets are finalized game content which are ready to be published. However, it can be used interchangeably with ‘Prefab’ as an asset is often composed of different types of prefabs.

Prefab
Prefabs can refer to the building blocks of an asset, for example the imported meshes of a custom asset are all ‘mesh prefabs’ or ‘render prefabs’. The asset itself an ‘asset prefab’ and so on. While assets are usually tangible, placeable objects a prefab can be a file that only appears in the UI, or as a texture, or as some other form of non-tangible item in the wider capabilities of the editor.

Component
Components are specific containers found in the inspector panel on the right of the editor. They are added to and make up the data of assets and prefabs. There are many different types of components available, each for a different and specific use.

#### Naming Conventions Matter

title: Asset Creation Guide
text:
the editor. They are added to and make up the data of assets and prefabs. There are many different types of components available, each for a different and specific use.

#### Naming Conventions Matter

The naming of your assets - whether the name of your FBX file, the mesh object, the object material, your textures or any other files used for importing - matter. The importer can only act on the information you provide to it in these naming conventions.

A typical asset name is formatted as follows:`<Theme>_<AssetName>_<Level>_<LotSize>_<Module>_<LOD>_<Material>`

title: Asset Creation Guide
text:
importer can only act on the information you provide to it in these naming conventions.

A typical asset name is formatted as follows:`<Theme>_<AssetName>_<Level>_<LotSize>_<Module>_<LOD>_<Material>`

- `<Theme>`- Current supported themes are “NA”, “EU” or no theme (leave it blank and don't have an _ before the assetname.)
- `<AssetName>`- is the actual asset name, CommercialHigh01 for example.
Beware of the numbering and the intentional abstinence of an underscore _ between name and number. Variations of a building often use a different ending number, this number is not considered by the pipeline and is part of the asset name itself.
- `<Level>`- is optional for spawnable buildings and can be L1 to L5 (usually only L1, L3 or L5).
It refers to the upgrade level of a building, C:SII supports 5 upgrades, usually 3 have their own assets, mid-levels are differently propped variations.
- `<LotSize>`- is optional and can be`#x#`where # is a number.
It refers to the grid dimension of the lot it fits in. For example 2x2, 2x4… etc
- `<Module>`- is optional and can be`Sub##`or`Ext##`where # is a number.
It refers to whether it is a base building or an Extension (extensions are additional buildings upgradable by the player in a preset location of the lot) or a Sub module (same as extension but placeable instead of preset to a specific location). For example Sub01, Ext03.
- `<LOD>`- is optional and is the intended LOD level for that asset. For example LOD1, LOD2
- `<Material>`- is optional and used with:

title: Asset Creation Guide
text:
tead of preset to a specific location). For example Sub01, Ext03.
- `<LOD>`- is optional and is the intended LOD level for that asset. For example LOD1, LOD2
- `<Material>`- is optional and used with:

- models (.fbx) to define the default desired material. For example: _Win for the window sub mesh. Check the sub mesh sections on a specific Asset Pipeline page.
 - textures (.png) to define the input texture slot. For example “_BaseColor”, “_ControlMask” etc...

For example:`NA_MyResidentialAsset01_L1_2x2.fbx`would be an appropriate naming convention for the main FBX of an asset.
If you need further examples, use the Asset Browser in the editor to find a built-in asset close to the intended use of yours to see how it is named.

#### Size, Scale, Triangle Count

title: Asset Creation Guide
text:
FBX of an asset.
If you need further examples, use the Asset Browser in the editor to find a built-in asset close to the intended use of yours to see how it is named.

#### Size, Scale, Triangle Count

- Common Sense approach is recommended when it comes to triangle count limits; while the editor does not have any hard-limit, it is important to optimize your assets as much as possible, depending on the size and use.
- As an asset creator, you should assess how the asset is intended to be used by players. Assets that are expected to be used often and more than once (zoned buildings, vehicles, vegetation/trees, props) should be optimised as much as possible, while balancing model accuracy. Assets such as service and signature buildings that will be used less often can afford a slightly higher triangle count.
- For example, zoned assets in the base game range from 3000 triangles to 95000 triangles, depending on their lot size and level, with a maximum of 6x6 cell lots and level 5. Some of the unique service buildings that are much bigger than the zoned building limit of 6x6 cells have over 100 000 triangles.
- Scale is 1:1 except when assets are enormous and need to be scaled down from their real-world counterparts to fit comfortably in game. The 1:1 scale is kept for the vertical axis to maintain the correct size compared to the characters. The average human height is considered 1.8m as a model reference.
- The System Units of the modelling software should be set to metric with 1 unit = 1 meter.
- Meshes can be modelled centered on the origin as the editor provides tools to move or rotate the mesh, enabling you to position it manually.
- Meshes must be FBX file format, 2018 version.

title: Asset Creation Guide
text:
1 meter.
- Meshes can be modelled centered on the origin as the editor provides tools to move or rotate the mesh, enabling you to position it manually.
- Meshes must be FBX file format, 2018 version.

#### Texel Density

- Texel Density is not specified per asset category. Texel Density of an asset depends on the object that is being textured.
- When UV mapping you should aim to have a similar texel density for every UV island across the asset.

- For complex shapes such as vehicles or larger buildings, it is contextual and depends on the art style of the asset.
 - Ideally, the priority on texel density should be given to the parts of the assets that are more important and visible most of the time from the player's perspective. You should avoid using very high texel density and too repetitive textures, or very low texel density that breaks the visual consistency of the asset compared to its surroundings. It is about finding the right balance.
 - If necessary, you can allocate a smaller texel density for elements that are less visible such as window frames, door handles or rain gutters.

#### Textures

title: Asset Creation Guide
text:
It is about finding the right balance.
 - If necessary, you can allocate a smaller texel density for elements that are less visible such as window frames, door handles or rain gutters.

#### Textures

- Texture sizes are outlined per asset category. To define the high quality texture standards for Cities Skylines II we recommend that these specifications are rarely exceeded, if at all.
- Textures should be 1:1 square ratio. Non-square textures are not supported.
- Textures should be a minimum of 512x512 pixels to be used by the virtual texturing process. Anything less than 512x512 is not supported. The maximum texture size is recommended not to exceed 4096x4096.
- Textures must be in .png file format.

# Asset Categories

The following pages contain detailed specifications and design best practices for each of the supported asset categories. This list will continue to expand.

#### Detailed Guidelines

title: Asset Creation Guide
text:
Asset Categories

The following pages contain detailed specifications and design best practices for each of the supported asset categories. This list will continue to expand.

#### Detailed Guidelines

- Buildings- instructions on the design principles and asset pipeline for buildings.
- Decals- Asset pipeline and design guides for creating decals ready to import into the editor.
- Props- instructions on the design principles and asset pipeline for props.
- Trees (Aging)- Asset pipeline and design guides for creating aging trees ready to import into the editor.
- Surfaces- Asset pipeline and design guide for creating custom surfaces, preparing textures to import into the editor.
Return to:Modding

title: Asset Pipeline: Aging Trees
text:
# Asset Pipeline: Aging Trees

Source: https://cs2.paradoxwikis.com/Asset_Pipeline:_Aging_Trees

Paradox logo black square Verified by Paradox Interactive
This article has been verified by Paradox Interactive for version 1.5.6 f1 of the game.

## Overview

Trees in Cities: Skylines II are divided into four vegetation layers:

- Trees — The dominant layer. Divided into City trees (smaller species, suited to lots and roadsides) and Wild trees (larger species, suited to parks and forests). The grouping only affects which species are selected by placeholder props that randomise between types — both groups can be placed freely by the player. Creators can add to the existing placeholders or create their own placeholder tree.
- Bushes — Divided into City and Wild. City bushes have a clean, trimmed appearance. Wild bushes have five life stages. No bushes have stump versions.
- Foliage High / Foliage Low — Undergrowth layers. No life stages, no grouping.

This guide focuses on Aging Trees only.

## Asset Design

### Age Stages

Each tree species has six separate meshes, one for each stage of its life:

Stage Naming Convention Notes

Child TreeNameChild01 Smallest form. Young sapling.

Teen TreeNameTeen01 Growing, transitional stage.

Adult TreeNameAdult01 Primary reference stage. Full mature form.

Elderly TreeNameElderly01 Ageing appearance. Slightly reduced or changed silhouette.

title: Asset Pipeline: Aging Trees
text:
Teen TreeNameTeen01 Growing, transitional stage.

Adult TreeNameAdult01 Primary reference stage. Full mature form.

Elderly TreeNameElderly01 Ageing appearance. Slightly reduced or changed silhouette.

Dead TreeNameDead01 No leaves. Bare branches only.

Stump TreeNameStump01 Created from the trunk of the Adult mesh. No leaves. No wind animation.

Each stage is a separate FBX file and a separate import folder. All six are required for a complete aging tree.

### Scale and Pivot

- Place the tree at the centre of your 3D scene with its pivot point at 0, 0, 0 in XYZ.
- Design each age stage to have an appropriate size that reflects natural growth progression. Having all six stages open simultaneously in your scene helps maintain consistent scale relationships.

### Topology

- Aim for clean quad-based topology. Use triangles only where they directly affect shading.
- Remove any edges or vertices that do not affect shading or UV layout.
- No n-gons.
- Add enough edge loops to cylindrical shapes so they read as smooth at the expected viewing distance.

### Materials

Trees require exactly two materials assigned within the 3D software before export. This is unique to tree assets — most other asset types use one material per mesh.

Slot Covers Shader Applied

0 Trunk and branches VegRoot

1 Foliage (leaves, needles, etc.) VegLeaves

title: Asset Pipeline: Aging Trees
text:
before export. This is unique to tree assets — most other asset types use one material per mesh.

Slot Covers Shader Applied

0 Trunk and branches VegRoot

1 Foliage (leaves, needles, etc.) VegLeaves

There is no required naming convention for the materials, but assign them in the correct slot order — slot 0 for trunk and slot 1 for foliage. The import pipeline uses the material slot index to determine which shader to apply to each part.

### UV Mapping

- All textures use power-of-two resolutions. UV islands must be placed within the 0.0 to 1.0 UV rectangle.
- Leave adequate spacing between UV islands to avoid mipmap bleed, especially on large surfaces.
- Overlapping UVs (mirroring) are acceptable where texture space efficiency requires it. Be aware that mirrored islands may cause issues with normal maps and may require additional bleed padding.
- Where possible, place mesh parts that use different colormask channels onto separate UV islands.

### Vertex Colors — Wind Animation

Trees use a vertex color system combined with a Wind Flutter Map texture to control how the geometry moves in the wind. The tree is decomposed into a hierarchy of three levels:

Level Green Channel Value Description

Trunk 0 Rests on the ground. Does not swing.

Branch Level A Between 0 and 1 (5–250 range, e.g. 5, 10, 15...) Connected to the trunk. Each branch must have its own unique value.

title: Asset Pipeline: Aging Trees
text:
nel Value Description

Trunk 0 Rests on the ground. Does not swing.

Branch Level A Between 0 and 1 (5–250 range, e.g. 5, 10, 15...) Connected to the trunk. Each branch must have its own unique value.

Branch Level B 1 (value 255) Connected to Level A branches. Highest frequency movement.

Important: The green channel is the only channel used for hierarchy assignment. Every branch in Level A must have a unique green channel value so the shader can identify individual branch pivot points. Using 5-step increments (5, 10, 15, 20...) across all Level A branches is a practical approach.

Paint the vertex colors directly on the geometry. Vertex colors must be included within the exported FBX file. Note: Stump meshes do not use wind animation and do not require vertex color hierarchy setup. The wind baking post-processor skips stump assets automatically.

### Imposter LODs

title: Asset Pipeline: Aging Trees
text:
the exported FBX file. Note: Stump meshes do not use wind animation and do not require vertex color hierarchy setup. The wind baking post-processor skips stump assets automatically.

### Imposter LODs

Aging trees use a unique import pipeline which automatically creates Imposter LOD models for your asset. No custom LOD is necessary to import for your different models. An Imposter is an extremely optimized sprite based LOD - a 2D billboard that uses pre-rendered images at multiple view angles. It replaces a 3D mesh at far distances. The billboard is oriented to always face the camera. Because it's just a texture sprite instead of detailed geometry, the GPU cost is near-zero compared to even a low-poly mesh. At close range you see the full 3D mesh; past a threshold the game swaps it for the impostor sprite.

## Textures

Trees use four textures per asset, plus a separate Wind Flutter Map. If you choose to share textures across age stages, they should be shared across all six stages — do not share selectively between only some.

In order to create a Cities: Skylines II compliant aging Tree asset, you need to produce the following texture types and import them into the game:

BaseColor,
ControlMask,
MaskMap,
Normal,
WindFlutterMap,

### BaseColor

Channel Channel Name Description

RGB BaseColor Diffuse Map The basic diffuse map for your mesh.

title: Asset Pipeline: Aging Trees
text:
nd import them into the game:

BaseColor,
ControlMask,
MaskMap,
Normal,
WindFlutterMap,

### BaseColor

Channel Channel Name Description

RGB BaseColor Diffuse Map The basic diffuse map for your mesh.

Alpha Opa city Defines which parts of the texture are alpha clipped. White is fully visible and Black is transparent. The alpha clip threshold is at 50% Black. It either renders as opaque or fully transparent.

Colormask interaction: The three colormask channels in the ControlMask multiply on top of the BaseColor in-game to add colour variation between individual trees. For this to work effectively, the BaseColor on foliage areas should be greyscale and as light as possible without losing surface detail. The lighter the BaseColor, the more control you have over the final in-game colour. Test this in your texture software by placing a colour layer in Multiply blend mode over your BaseColor.

Naming convention: TreeName_BaseColor.png

### MaskMap Note: The tree MaskMap uses a different channel layout to the standard building MaskMap. Do not apply building shader MaskMap conventions to trees.

Channel Channel Name Description

title: Asset Pipeline: Aging Trees
text:
r.png

### MaskMap Note: The tree MaskMap uses a different channel layout to the standard building MaskMap. Do not apply building shader MaskMap conventions to trees.

Channel Channel Name Description

R Surface Shine Paint leaf and branch surfaces to give them a natural waxy or glossy quality. Black areas have no sheen and will not respond to rain. Mid-range values (as used in the example Oak) give a subtle natural shine in dry conditions and a visible wet, reflective appearance during rain. Avoid full white — it produces an unnaturally metallic look in dry conditions. Leave background and alpha-clipped areas black.

G Snow Remove Prevents snow accumulation on surfaces painted white. Not currently functional — Leave black or set as needed for future compatibility.

B Default Black This channel is not currently used and should remain black.

Alpha Glossiness Defines the glossiness of the diffuse. White is very glossy, Black is not glossy at all. In the standard PBR workflow, glossiness is the inverse of roughness.

Naming convention: TreeName_MaskMap.png

### ControlMask

The ControlMask is a texture unique to vegetation assets. It drives in-game colour variation.

Channel Channel Name Description

R Colormask 1 Black and white mask. Areas painted white will be tinted by Colour Variation set 1 (Channel 0) in-game.

title: Asset Pipeline: Aging Trees
text:
ion assets. It drives in-game colour variation.

Channel Channel Name Description

R Colormask 1 Black and white mask. Areas painted white will be tinted by Colour Variation set 1 (Channel 0) in-game.

G Colormask 2 Areas painted white will be tinted by Colour Variation set 2 (Channel 1) in-game.

B Colormask 3 Areas painted white will be tinted by Colour Variation set 3 (Channel 2) in-game.

Colormask channels apply colour as a multiply on top of the BaseColor in the areas marked white. The three channels must not overlap — a pixel must only be white in one channel at a time. It is not required to use all three channels, but using all three gives more colour variation between individual trees in-game. Apply the masks primarily to foliage UV islands.

Naming convention: TreeName_ControlMask.png

### Normal

Channel Description

RGB Tangent-space normal map. Use OpenGL convention (Y-up, blue-purple). Export from your software in linear colour space, not sRGB.

Naming convention: TreeName_Normal.png

### Wind Flutter Map

The Wind Flutter Map is a separate texture used by the import pipeline during wind baking. It helps define where the pivot point of each branch is within the texture space, particularly for foliage cards (alpha-cutout leaf polygons).

- Simple black and red texture. Black = no pivot effect, red = pivot location.
- Added to the same folder as the other textures.

title: Asset Pipeline: Aging Trees
text:
re space, particularly for foliage cards (alpha-cutout leaf polygons).

- Simple black and red texture. Black = no pivot effect, red = pivot location.
- Added to the same folder as the other textures.

Naming convention: TreeName_WindFlutterMap.png

Tip: You can find example files in your installation folder: `\Cities Skylines II\Cities2_Data\Content\Game\.ModdingToolchain\ExampleAssets\ExampleAgingTree`

## Sharing Textures Across Age Stages

Sharing textures between age stages saves disk space and reduces the number of unique texture assets. The settings.json sharedAssets system handles this entirely at import time.

Recommended sharing strategy:

- Child, Teen, Elderly — Share the Adult stage's textures. The foliage appearance and bark texture are typically the same across these stages; only the mesh shape and scale differ with age.
- Dead — Share the Adult stage's textures. The Dead stage has no leaves, but the bark texture is the same — the absence of foliage geometry is what distinguishes it visually, not a different texture set.
- Stump — Share the Adult stage's textures. The stump is modelled from the Adult trunk, so the textures match directly.

How to set it up in settings.json — the key is the filename the importer expects to find in the current folder (formatted as ThisStage_TextureType.png), and the value is the relative path to the actual file:

title: Asset Pipeline: Aging Trees
text:
set it up in settings.json — the key is the filename the importer expects to find in the current folder (formatted as ThisStage_TextureType.png), and the value is the relative path to the actual file:

```
"sharedAssets": {
 "MyOakTreeChild01_BaseColor.png": "../MyOakTreeAdult01/MyOakTreeAdult01_BaseColor.png",
 "MyOakTreeChild01_ControlMask.png": "../MyOakTreeAdult01/MyOakTreeAdult01_ControlMask.png",
 "MyOakTreeChild01_MaskMap.png": "../MyOakTreeAdult01/MyOakTreeAdult01_MaskMap.png",
 "MyOakTreeChild01_Normal.png": "../MyOakTreeAdult01/MyOakTreeAdult01_Normal.png",
 "MyOakTreeChild01_WindFlutterMap.png": "../MyOakTreeAdult01/MyOakTreeAdult01_WindFlutterMap.png"
}

```

The path prefix ../ goes up one directory level from the current age subfolder into the parent folder, then into the target age subfolder. No physical copies of the texture files are needed in the Child folder.

## Import Folder Structure

Each age stage must be in its own subfolder. All six stages for a single species sit inside one parent folder, located inside your ProjectFiles directory. The following assumes you are sharing textures across all included meshes.

title: Asset Pipeline: Aging Trees
text:
own subfolder. All six stages for a single species sit inside one parent folder, located inside your ProjectFiles directory. The following assumes you are sharing textures across all included meshes.

```
ProjectFiles/
└── MyOakTree/
 ├── MyOakTreeAdult01/
 │ ├── MyOakTreeAdult01.fbx
 │ ├── MyOakTreeAdult01_BaseColor.png
 │ ├── MyOakTreeAdult01_ControlMask.png
 │ ├── MyOakTreeAdult01_MaskMap.png
 │ ├── MyOakTreeAdult01_Normal.png
 │ └── MyOakTreeAdult01_WindFlutterMap.png
 ├── MyOakTreeChild01/
 │ ├── MyOakTreeChild01.fbx
 │ └── settings.json
 ├── MyOakTreeTeen01/
 │ ├── MyOakTreeTeen01.fbx
 │ └── settings.json
 ├── MyOakTreeElderly01/
 │ ├── MyOakTreeElderly01.fbx
 │ └── settings.json
 ├── MyOakTreeDead01/
 │ ├── MyOakTreeDead01.fbx
 │ └── settings.json
 └── MyOakTreeStump01/
 ├── MyOakTreeStump01.fbx
 └── settings.json

```
Once your source files are ready to import, continue to: Assets: Import and Setup Aging TreesReturn to: Asset Creation Guide

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
# Asset Pipeline: Buildings

Source: https://cs2.paradoxwikis.com/Asset_Pipeline:_Buildings

Verified by Paradox Interactive
This article has been verified by Paradox Interactive for version 1.5.2 f1 of the game.

## Asset Design - Buildings

### Zoned Buildings

Zoned Buildings in Cities: Skylines II spawn after the player paints a zonetype on the side of the road. Here's a full list of supported zone types:

ResidentialCommercialOfficeIndustrial

Low DensityLow Density
(incl. Gas Stations, Motels)Low DensityManufacturing
(incl.
Warehouses)

Medium Density Row HousingHigh Density
(incl. Hotels)High Density

Medium Density

High Density

Mixed Housing

Low Rent Housing Note: While the above table lists all currently supported zone types, custom zones can be created - and while they can be complex - they can be customized in various ways.

#### Level Progression of Zoned Buildings

Zoned buildings progress from Level 1 to 5. Building Levels are intended to represent the progression of the building and its occupants. From the lowest to the highest level;

- The building quality improves,
- The upkeep costs get increased,
- The rents get increased,
- The wealth level of occupants (residents or companies) increases.

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
From the lowest to the highest level;

- The building quality improves,
- The upkeep costs get increased,
- The rents get increased,
- The wealth level of occupants (residents or companies) increases.

Base game zoned buildings contain 3 unique meshes for levels 1, 3 and 5, with level 2 and 4 featuring non-visual upgrades. It is possible to create one unique mesh per level and demonstrate level progression visually across all 5 levels.

Below is a list of ways we have differentiated between Building levels:

- Size of the building mesh compared to lot (structure is smaller on lower levels)
- Complexity of shape/topology (simpler on lower levels)
- The amount of floors (lower level building have less floors)
- Decoration amount in windows and window frames (simpler window frames on lower levels)
- Decoration amount in doors and door frames (simpler door frames on lower levels)
- Roof windows or dormers (no roof windows on lower levels)
- Gutters (no gutters on lower levels)
- Chimney cowls (no chimney cowls on lower levels)
- Terraces and porches (no terraces and porches on lower levels)
- Material type and expense in reality (more wood and raw bricks used on lower levels)
- Material cleanliness (less clean on lower levels)

If you re-use a mesh across Building Levels there are other ways to add variation. For example:

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
ity (more wood and raw bricks used on lower levels)
- Material cleanliness (less clean on lower levels)

If you re-use a mesh across Building Levels there are other ways to add variation. For example:

- color variations on the roof, walls and other features.
- Re-propping the same mesh with a different set of props and saving as a second version.
- Position and rotation of the mesh on the lot.

#### Triangle Counts for Zoned Buildings

There is no hard-limit that the game imposes, though you may find it useful to have an indication of the current triangle counts used in the base game buildings, for a best practice approach for your own meshes.

The table below shows the triangle average of the base game zones and a soft maximum number of triangles.

- Triangle Averageis showing the average number of triangles in the main meshes of buildings that exist in that zone, excluding any extreme outliers and excluding any sub meshes (like _Win or _Gls etc).
- The Soft Maximumis a rounded figure of the highest number of triangles in a single building's main mesh in that zone.

ZonetypeTriangle Average (excl outliers)Soft Triangle Maximum

Low Density Housing800015000

Low Density Business

Low Density Offices

Medium Density Row Housing600014000

Mixed Housing (Excluding Storefront)1500028000

Medium Density Housing3500075000

High Density Business1400030000

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
15000

Low Density Business

Low Density Offices

Medium Density Row Housing600014000

Mixed Housing (Excluding Storefront)1500028000

Medium Density Housing3500075000

High Density Business1400030000

High Density Housing5000095000

High Density Offices4000078000

Specialized Industry Hubs2000062000

Industrial Manufacturing30006000

Industrial Warehouses

Industrial Storage1500035000

Low Rent Housing1500025000Note: These numbers are indicative only and do not represent a hard limit, nor do they represent a predetermined target number of triangles for that zone.

When creating a custom zone type, refer to the base game examples to estimate an appropriate average and soft-maximum triangle count for your buildings. Make sure you produce a wide range of lot sizes to handle all roadside cell variations in depth and width, and provide at least one complete level 1–5 set for every lot size you include. You can add extra level variations once you have a full set.

#### Lot Sizes for Zoned Buildings

Zoned buildings must adhere to minimum and maximum lot sizes. Note: Lot sizes are listed as Width x Depth, where width measures the number of cells wide along a road length and depth measures how many cells deep away from the side of the road.

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
maximum lot sizes. Note: Lot sizes are listed as Width x Depth, where width measures the number of cells wide along a road length and depth measures how many cells deep away from the side of the road.

- One cell is 8x8m.
- Minimum lot size is 1x2 (8x16m)
- Maximum lot size is 6x6 (48x48m)
- For lot sizes, remember that the zone algorithm always tries to spawn the largest possible lot. It first selects the deepest option, then the widest, from the available choices. For example, if a 4x4 space is available and you provide two 2x2 assets but only one 4x4 asset, the algorithm will still prioritize the 4x4. Keep this in mind when creating zone-type packs, especially if most of your assets use smaller lot sizes.
Note: As land value changes during gameplay, the zoning algorithm will adapt by subdividing larger plot areas into smaller lots to account for the higher rent associated with increased land value..

### Service Buildings

Service Buildings are assets that can be placed by players and aim to have a realistic scale. Some Service buildings come with upgrades. There's three types of upgrades in Cities: Skylines II: Operational, Extensions and Sub-Buildings.

#### Size, Scale and Triangle Counts of Service Buildings

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
Some Service buildings come with upgrades. There's three types of upgrades in Cities: Skylines II: Operational, Extensions and Sub-Buildings.

#### Size, Scale and Triangle Counts of Service Buildings

The maximum possible lot size in Cities: Skylines II is 1000x1000 cells. When selecting the appropriate lot size for your building, the larger it is, the more difficult it will become to be placed in a variety of circumstances in a city.

If the service building is being made to match a specific zone style, it is recommended to pick a lot size that can seamlessly blend-in with the rest of the zone you wish to support.

For service buildings featuring vehicle bays and garages consider the following:

- You can base your garage door / vehicle bay door dimensions after base game vehicles, but make sure they are still relatively close to the real life dimensions.

- The minimum width for a vehicle is 3m wide.
 - The maximum height clearance for a vehicle is 4.6m before touching any tram wires.

Helicopter pads have larger “airspace” collision boxes around the pad marker. This is important to consider for service buildings with low building height, as it can block nearby zones from developing.

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
elicopter pads have larger “airspace” collision boxes around the pad marker. This is important to consider for service buildings with low building height, as it can block nearby zones from developing.

When it comes to the triangle count, consider how many times your service building can realistically be placed by players. A very small, minimalist fire station can be placed many times and as such, it might be important to optimise its triangle count. A large train station inspired by a historic European station would likely be placed only once, which justifies a higher triangle budget - but it would be appropriate to consider making the building unique.

#### Building Upgrades

Service buildings may feature a building upgrade system introduced with Cities: Skylines II. There are three types of upgrades: Operational, Extensions and Sub-Buildings. Operational Upgrades

They add functional and statistical changes to the building without changing its looks. These can be bandwidth increases for the Radio Mast, exhaust filters installed in a Coal Power Plant, or a dispatch centre built inside the Taxi Depot. Operational upgrades do not require any mesh or texture. Extensions

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
dth increases for the Radio Mast, exhaust filters installed in a Coal Power Plant, or a dispatch centre built inside the Taxi Depot. Operational upgrades do not require any mesh or texture. Extensions

These are visible upgrades placed directly on the lot of the main asset - instead of outside of the original lot. They usually increase the building’s capa city for vehicles, output, range, or the number of citizens being serviced.

These include, among other things, extension wings for medical facilities and schools providing more patient and student capa city, respectively, garages for various services increasing the size of their service vehicle fleet and additional turbines for power plants increasing their electricity output and fuel consumption.

Extensions have a predetermined, fixed position. Extensions are unique and can only be placed once per asset, occupying that fixed position. Assets can have multiple extensions that can be placed on different positions on the main building. Extensions should be designed to work mutually exclusive of each other. Extensions can delete props from areas on which they are placed. This allows creators to fully prop a main building and players use them without the obligation of using the extensions.

It is currently not possible to have extensions that forbid the placement of another. Sub-buildings

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
to fully prop a main building and players use them without the obligation of using the extensions.

It is currently not possible to have extensions that forbid the placement of another. Sub-buildings

Sub-buildings are separate buildings attached to or placed in a radius around the service building’s existing lot. They can increase, for example, the fuel storage capa city of power plants or add new functionality to a building, such as the Children’s Clinic and playground upgrades for the Elementary School.

Sub-buildings naturally require additional space to be built so it is important to take into consideration the placement of the service building and make sure that there is enough free space around them for those potential sub-buildings added in the future. Sub-buildings can be placed more freely and thus can be created as their own buildings without the limitations of the parent building. (they are not comparable to Sub Buildings in Cities: Skylines). The road side of the Sub-Building is the side that will always face the center of the lot when snapping to the main lot is active.

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
ding. (they are not comparable to Sub Buildings in Cities: Skylines). The road side of the Sub-Building is the side that will always face the center of the lot when snapping to the main lot is active.

It is possible to adjust the range inside which sub-buildings can be placed. It is also possible to force sub-buildings to be placed only on the perimeter of the main building (such as Harbor). You can create sub-buildings (and extensions) that might require another sub-building or extension as a requirement. It is possible to create unique sub-buildings, which can be placed only once.

### Signature Buildings

If there's a building with stunning details and striking style, don't worry. We've got you covered. All zone types have matching Signature buildings In Cities: Skylines II. Signature buildings are what we can consider 'unique' buildings, featuring a distinct architecture style or a striking design making them stand out from zonable or service buildings. They are intended as anchor points of the city's skyline, giving it a special look.

#### Size, Scale and Triangle Counts of Signature Buildings

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
aking them stand out from zonable or service buildings. They are intended as anchor points of the city's skyline, giving it a special look.

#### Size, Scale and Triangle Counts of Signature Buildings

Signature buildings can be anything from small diner to large hotel complex, but as long as they have matching zones in the game, they will work as intended. Signature buildings are placed by the player so they don't have the same lot size limitations than the spawning ones. As signature buildings can only be placed once, the triangle soft maximum for them is higher than zoned or service buildings.

#### In-game Features of Signature Buildings

Aside from their artistic significance, signature buildings are still functional assets and they are part of the simulation. Signature buildings in base game do not have any construction costs or upkeep on placement, however this can be adjusted for custom assets by asset creators. Signature buildings can also provide one or multiple effects that can boost a certain function in the city simulation. These effects can be positive or negative, local effects with range or city-wide.

A building asset consists of a main mesh, any sub meshes and LOD meshes.

## Asset Pipeline - Buildings

A building asset consists of a main mesh, any sub meshes and LOD meshes.

Main Mesh

It consists of the basic architectural elements of the building. It acts as the parent mesh for all of the sub meshes associated with it.
Sub mesheshave to match the position, rotation and name of its parent mesh. Current collection of supported sub meshes and their required suffixes:

- Windows: Featuring parallax interior rooms with two versions.
- Glass: Clear transparent glass.
- Grass: Theme terrain based grass.
- Water: Moving water surface, semi transparent.

LOD (Level of Detail) Meshes
These are lower poly versions of the main mesh. Cities: Skylines II building pipeline requires 2 LODs, varying in complexity and texture size for an efficient rendering performance. These meshes are called:

- LOD1: Simplified version of the main mesh, sharing the same texture.
- LOD2: Very low poly, baked-in mesh that gets triggered on a far distance.

### Main Mesh

#### Overview

Before starting the modelling process, the content creator should:

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
sharing the same texture.
- LOD2: Very low poly, baked-in mesh that gets triggered on a far distance.

### Main Mesh

#### Overview

Before starting the modelling process, the content creator should:

- Properly research the building and decide which visual features are the most important to model, and/or represent them on the texture.
- Decide on the purpose of the building. A growable building will have lower triangle count maximum compared to a signature asset. If it is a service building, it might require adjustments to allow service vehicles to spawn.
- Decide on the lot size. If it is a growable building it must adhere to minimum and maximum lot sizes.

#### Modelling Conventions

In Cities: Skylines II the amount of modelled details can be higher than in its predecessor without going too far over the top. For example windows should be inset, window frames can be modelled if there are window frames and roof eaves should have interesting details.

Windows, window frames, doors, door handles, rain gutters, chimneys and other details that are related to architectural style of the building should always be modelled, whereas more generic additions like air conditioning units, fire-escapes, ventilation pipes, mailboxes etc. are best made as props.

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
ed to architectural style of the building should always be modelled, whereas more generic additions like air conditioning units, fire-escapes, ventilation pipes, mailboxes etc. are best made as props.

It is recommended to keep the object pivot at the bottom-center as much as possible. This is for your own convenience, as it mitigates messy situations with sub meshes and extensions later. The asset can be modelled in the center of the scene in both horizontal axes and moved and rotated into its correct place in the Editor or it can be modelled in place. The building's "ground" level is always exactly on 0 on the vertical axis. Note: Rotating your mesh can lead to unintended issues for animations, best practice is to have the rotation of your mesh correct on import.

The game will add a foundation below ground based on the vertices and edges intersecting the terrain at y=0. If your asset features a visible underground section, it is recommended not to leave any edge at 0, but they should be moved above or under 0 to prevent foundation generation. Modelling downward facing polygons at y=0 cancel the generation of the automatic foundation.

#### Tips on Optimisation

Optimizing your model is still best practice. We recommend using normal mapping for details that are less than 5cm in depth (on average - some exceptions would be considered acceptable).

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
Optimisation

Optimizing your model is still best practice. We recommend using normal mapping for details that are less than 5cm in depth (on average - some exceptions would be considered acceptable).

The "5cm" is not a hard rule. Depending on the size of the building and complexity, you might find yourself in a situation where optimisation will be necessary even for details larger than 5cm. This will be the part where as an asset creator you will have to prioritize the importance of the visual identity of the building vs. the costs of labour and topology for performance impact.

To keep less labour intensive during the production of the asset, below is the recommended list of areas you can optimize on the model:

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
ng vs. the costs of labour and topology for performance impact.

To keep less labour intensive during the production of the asset, below is the recommended list of areas you can optimize on the model:

- You can intersect meshes without creating additional topology. An example would be a steel beam being connected to a façade. You can just move the beam further inside the model instead of joining two topologies together.
- A door handle will be around 5cm extruded from a door surface and therefore should be modelled. Any other detail such as keyhole should be on the normal map if they are to be featured.
- The panels of a door may be 5cm deep, therefore they would be modelled, if however they are less than 5cm then a normal map may suffice.
- The average door frame could be 5cm (compared to the window glass surface) and would be modelled.
- The ridges in an architectural cornice may be less than 5cm and would therefore be normally mapped on a single face, rather than modelled.
- The silhouette should be taken into account when considering detail - most details under 5cm depth do not change the silhouette of the model. If you would notice a distinct change in the silhouette then the detail should be modelled.
- Exceptions may include thin fencing - where you may alpha map the majority of the fence and model only the support framework to create visual depth.
- Things like ornamented façade details (like carved motifs or statuettes) can use a combination of modelling and normal mapping techniques to convey the details.
- For buildings that have a lot of window frames, you can intersect frames into each other to conserve triangles.
- Angle based auto-smooth is a great tool to take advantage of in any modelling software, it can allow you to model curved objects at lower costs of triangle count. You should aim for at least 60 degrees of auto smoothing, helping you to optimize curved details such as rain gutters, circular door handles or columns.
- Any detail that can be a prop, should be considered as a prop. While you can make your own, the game provides a wide range of props which you can use as well.

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
ters, circular door handles or columns.
- Any detail that can be a prop, should be considered as a prop. While you can make your own, the game provides a wide range of props which you can use as well.

#### UV Mapping of Main Mesh

- UV range has to be between 0 and 1 and spacing between UV islands should be large enough to avoid issues with mipmapping.
- The space needs to be even larger on the islands where mirrored UV's are used.
- Overlapping UV maps should be used whenever it is possible to save texture space. UV maps should be always laid out so that different materials are separated with empty space to avoid any bleed across UV islands. It is recommended to cluster similarly colored materials together to prevent bleeding between very dark and very light materials. If the contrast of color is too large, it is best to increase the space between two islands to prevent bleeding. Depending on your texture workflow, you can stack uv islands which should help keeping a higher texel density across the board, provided that textures do not feature any repetitive visual elements.

#### Naming Convention for Main Mesh

- It is important to name your main mesh clearly, as it will be the bases for naming materials, textures and sub meshes.
- Mesh object names in the 3D software must be identical with the filename.
- Mesh names and suffixes are case sensitive.

- Mesh names and suffixes are case sensitive.

### Sub Meshes

Windows, glass surfaces, terrain and water surfaces that use special shaders in the game need to be modelled, UV-mapped and exported separately with an identical pivot to the main mesh. UV-mapping in most of the sub meshes can be done via auto UV-map on the modelling software of choice, with minimum amount of distortion. In some cases -such as windows- the orientation of UV islands matter. Sub meshes rely on shaders so there is no need for asset creators to create additional sets of textures for sub meshes.

Sub meshes in Cities: Skylines are not comparable to "sub-meshes" in Cities: Skylines. Windows Sub Meshes

There are currently two window sub mesh variants and shaders, offering parallax based interior/room generation:

- Windows with opaque windows with interior map) =_Win
- Windows with opaque milk windows with blurred interior map) =_Wim
Note: While technically recognized by the game, the _Wim sub mesh parallax interior is currently identical to _Win.

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
erior map) =_Win
- Windows with opaque milk windows with blurred interior map) =_Wim
Note: While technically recognized by the game, the _Wim sub mesh parallax interior is currently identical to _Win.

Cities: Skylines II references the UV Map of windows sub meshes to generate parallax rooms as well as night lighting. The mapping and the distribution of windows directly influences the shader.

The game currently supports 25 variations of rooms per UV Map. These rooms can feature different curtain types and alignments as well as light on/off states that are randomized based on building occupancy (except 2 variations that are simply on/off all times). Parallax interiors are assigned based on checksum on mesh names. The editor will assign different interiors based on the following words if they are included in your mesh name:

- Industrial
- Office
- ResidentialHigh
- ResidentialLow
- CommercialHigh
- CommercialLow

If the mesh name is custom, it will be using an office variant.

Modelling Windows Sub Meshes
The process of modelling windows is relatively straightforward:

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
ntialLow
- CommercialHigh
- CommercialLow

If the mesh name is custom, it will be using an office variant.

Modelling Windows Sub Meshes
The process of modelling windows is relatively straightforward:

- Windows that use the interior mapping shader should be modelled separately from the main mesh.
- Don't use a single polygon behind several windows, Separate them as shown to the right. A single polygon behind a window that has several frames is okay.
- Make sure the window sub mesh is properly aligned with the window frames on the main mesh. You can use snapping options in the 3D software of your choice to align edges of the window faces to the frames. Alternatively, you can scale up windows a little to ensure windows sub mesh is well covering the empty space right behind the frames.

UV Mapping of Windows Sub Meshes

In order to make UV mapping convenient for asset creators, we provide a reference texture which will help you with windows UV mapping. While the game does not directly use this reference texture, it calculates night time illumination and interior room generation based on the location of UV islands on the UV coordinates.

There are 25 different color values in the texture:

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
is reference texture, it calculates night time illumination and interior room generation based on the location of UV islands on the UV coordinates.

There are 25 different color values in the texture:

- Always on = When building is functional, these windows will always be lit at night
- Room 01-23 = When the building is functional, these windows will get randomized if they are lit or not at night.
- Always off = These windows will never be lit, even when the building is functional.

Asset creators can control the interior parallax "rooms" in the asset by UV-mapping different windows to different color value areas on the reference texture. Window UV's should always be placed logically to give the impression of rooms inside the asset.

UV Mapping Guidelines for Windows Sub Meshes

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
s to different color value areas on the reference texture. Window UV's should always be placed logically to give the impression of rooms inside the asset.

UV Mapping Guidelines for Windows Sub Meshes

- Asset UV's should always use as many rooms as logically possible to give more variation and control over the asset.
- UV islands can be placed over the red marker text in the reference texture.
- The UV island orientation of windows needs to always match the world orientation of the corresponding polygon where the top edge of the window polygon is up in UV space.
- Overlapping UV's for windows are forbidden.
- Size of the window UV islands doesn't matter (but using maximum space available is still recommended).
- UV island correct aspect ratio should be respected - do not squish or stretch your windows, but scale them down if necessary.

When the building is imported to the game, the final room layout will be created using this UV mapping as a starting point. If two windows that are in close logical proximity to each other in 3D space, and are mapped to the same room on the reference texture the game will calculate them as looking into the same single interior space.

Naming Convention for Windows Sub Meshes

- Windows sub meshes need to be named after the main mesh and they need to have a suffix designating their shaders.
- Mesh names and suffixes are case sensitive.

Glass Shader is used to imitate glass surfaces. Similar to window parallax, it is handled by the game and the asset creator doesn't need to provide any texture.

Modelling & UV Mapping Guidelines for Glass Sub Mesh

- Glass sub meshes should be modelled separately from the main mesh.
- Best practice for modelling glass would be to add some thickness - in other words, avoid using single planes for glass sub meshes. The glass shader is intended to be single sided and will not render on back faces.
- As for the UV mapping, glass sub mesh can be auto UV mapped. The current shader features some smears and dirt on the glass; if you have a large glass canopy made of out polygons, it is recommended to UV map manually instead.
- Overlapping UV islands is forbidden.

Grass shader is available for buildings and props to use terrain texture on buildings and props. The sub mesh acts like a continuation of the terrain texture. The mesh will sample the terrain texture on the terrain below it and project it up onto the surface of the sub mesh.

Modelling & UV Mapping Guidelines for Grass Sub Mesh

- UV unwrap with minimal distortion.
- Overlapping UV islands is forbidden.
- Auto UV unwrap can be used, as this shader is projecting the terrain texture.

The water shader is used to create pools or fountains. The shader is semi-transparent, and has a loop animation to mimic the water surface.

Modelling & UV Mapping Guidelines for Water Sub Mesh

- UV unwrap with minimal distortion.
- Overlapping UV islands is forbidden.
- Consider maximizing the UV space as the scale of the water texture depends on the size of the UV islands.
- If you have a polygonal surface for water, consider manual UV unwrapping instead and do not split the uv topology.
- As this is a semi-transparent shader, you must model any surface on the main mesh which will be rendered under this sub mesh.
Note: If the water sub mesh is enclosed with another sub mesh using the glass shader, water will not be rendered.

Naming Convention for Water Sub Mesh

- Water sub mesh needs to be named after the main mesh and it needs to have a suffix designating its shader.
- Mesh names and suffixes are case sensitive.

### Textures

Buildings in Cities: Skylines II uses a variation of the standard PBR pipeline (with metallic/glossiness), with custom additions in order to cover the game’s special needs.

In order to create a building for Cities: Skylines II, you need to produce the following texture types and import into the game:

BaseColor,
ControlMask,
MaskMap,
Normal,
Emissive.

- The resolution of textures should be no more than 4096x4096 pixels, and no less than 512x512 pixels.
- All textures must be .png and having identical resolutions.
- The game uses virtual texturing and will automatically downsize texture resolution based on the user's settings.
- If you have multiple buildings which can share components and building elements, you can texture them together with a single set of textures. This is called texture sharing and how to import assets sharing textures is explained in the Importing section.

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
ents and building elements, you can texture them together with a single set of textures. This is called texture sharing and how to import assets sharing textures is explained in the Importing section.

- Texture sharing works at its most optimised when textures are shared across assets that will generally be in view of the camera together and therefore rendered at the same time. A good example of this is a service building and its upgrades sharing textures as they are usually placed within close proximity to each other.

- SnowRemove (alpha channel on ControlMask) will be used to control snow accumulation on the surface of the asset. Mapping UV islands to distinguish between snow and SnowRemove should be considered. SnowRemove should be applied to surfaces like terraces, stairs and balconies and any areas that have roofing over them. Applying SnowRemove to a UV island 'removes' or forbids snow accumulation.
Note: SnowRemove is not yet implemented, so any use of the SnowRemove channel is for futureproofing your asset.
- The art for the Base Game is specifically a "clean" visual style, with minimal grunge or aging effects.
- You can of course add weather and grunge/dirt to your custom assets. Make sure to consider the amount of grunge and aging, and that they will not cause repetitive patterns on the textures, especially on rooftops and walls.

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
weather and grunge/dirt to your custom assets. Make sure to consider the amount of grunge and aging, and that they will not cause repetitive patterns on the textures, especially on rooftops and walls.

Depending on what software you use, you can create your own textures with the following channel structure to support building creation:

RGBBaseColor Diffuse MapThe basic diffuse map for your mesh.

AlphaOpa cityDefines which parts of the texture are alpha clipped. White is fully visible and Black is transparent. The alpha clip threshold is at 50% Black. It either renders as opaque or fully transparent.

Where each color mask is applied to your asset, the game will use it to assign one of the 3 color variations once imported. See also:Assets: Setting Up Color Variations

GCoatApplies an extra shine, or glossy coat to the diffuse on top of the standard glossiness. White is a full coat, Black is no coat.

AlphaGlossinessDefines the glossiness of the diffuse. White is very glossy, Black is not glossy at all. In the standard PBR workflow, glossiness is the inverse of roughness.

Emissive
The game supports two, mutually exclusive Emissive art pipelines, creators should choose the most appropriate for their asset:

- Single Emissive sources
- Multiple Emissive sources

Single EmissiveThis is where the game expects a single controllable emissive source, which can select a single purpose from a range of different uses. The most common use being ‘decorative light’.

ChannelChannel NameDescription

RGBEmissive MapApplies an emissive effect to the building, color and brightness is set through the RGB values on this map.

Naming Convention:ExampleAsset_Emissive.png

Multiple EmissiveThis is where the game allows advanced control over multiple emissive sources. This is made functional with the combined use of Emissive textures and Emissive ID textures. Note: When UV mapping the Multiple Emissive textures, there can be a maximum of 4 lights per triangles, or triangle neighbours if the vertices are shared across multiple triangles that get light contribution so be cautious when authoring emissive or tessellate the model accordingly. As a rule of thumb, more than 4 lights on any one UV island will likely cause problems.

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
riangles that get light contribution so be cautious when authoring emissive or tessellate the model accordingly. As a rule of thumb, more than 4 lights on any one UV island will likely cause problems.

An Emissive texture and Emissive ID texture work together as a pair, where the game can recognize up to 4 pairs of Emissive and Emissive ID textures. The textures are named and paired as follows:

ChannelChannel NameDescription

RGBEmissive MapApplies an emissive effect to the building, color and brightness is set through the RGB values on this map.

Emissive ID Textures are used to encode a light ID from 1 to 255. For convenience, you can choose whatever value you wish, for example every tenth, so that you can tell them apart more easily. Using two values that are right next to each other is not recommended since they seem to become merged into one in some cases. 0 is always no light.

ChannelChannel NameDescription

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
apart more easily. Using two values that are right next to each other is not recommended since they seem to become merged into one in some cases. 0 is always no light.

ChannelChannel NameDescription

GrayscaleEmissive ID MapPixel-perfect masking of different light sources, or light IDs. If a pixel is set to 10 it will be assigned a light ID different to a pixel set to 20. By assigning pixels that are the same coordinates to the Emissive texture the same gray value on the Texture ID will result in the game treating those pixels as the same light source, and therefore control the emissive of those pixels together. Note:

- If multiple lights overlap the same pixel areas, then another emissive map is needed, up to 4 maps. Even when using a single map, the maximum of 4 lights per triangle applies.
- Emissive Textures may use infinite dilation, but Emissive ID textures must not, and must be pixel-perfect, no anti-aliasing or blending to 0.

### LOD Meshes

LOD meshes are lower poly representations of the original mesh topology. They are triggered when the camera moves away from the building location. The threshold to trigger LOD2 meshes depends on the user graphics settings, size of the mesh and the size of the lot.

LOD1 Mesh and Textures

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
red when the camera moves away from the building location. The threshold to trigger LOD2 meshes depends on the user graphics settings, size of the mesh and the size of the lot.

LOD1 Mesh and Textures

LOD1 is the intermediate LOD between main mesh and far LOD2. This is usually the mesh which most of the players will see from the usual distance city-builder games are being played. Because of this, it should be made out of main mesh, however it should feature less details and mesh complexity.

- The triangle count should be less than 50% of the main mesh’s triangle count.
- Small mesh features such as door handles, ornaments, rooftop details, pipes, rain gutters can be manually removed from LOD1 mesh.
- Window and door frames should be simplified while retaining the overall topology.
- For a quick result, you can use a decimate modifier of the 3D software of your choice and reduce the polycount via the software algorithm.
Note: If you are using any modifier to simplify your main mesh, make sure the UV mapping is not being severely altered in the process. Be sure to keep as much of the original silhouette as possible to avoid popping as the LOD level changes.

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
implify your main mesh, make sure the UV mapping is not being severely altered in the process. Be sure to keep as much of the original silhouette as possible to avoid popping as the LOD level changes.

- LOD1 Mesh shares textures with the main mesh.
- LOD1 main mesh requires LOD1 sub meshes as well. The process explained above applies to sub meshes except windows sub meshes.
- UV mapping of windows sub meshes defines parallax shader features, so you should avoid simplifying the topology.
Note: When simplifying the main mesh into LOD1, take care around the areas the _Win sub mesh should appear, you should avoid topology that could create gaps or otherwise cover up the sub mesh. See also:Texturing Sharing

LOD2 Mesh and Textures

LOD2, is the far LOD. It gets triggered when the distance between camera and building is very high, and just rendering its silhouette is enough. As a result, the triangle count for LOD2 meshes are very low, and they require a separate custom mesh and textures, which can be baked-in.

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
ng is very high, and just rendering its silhouette is enough. As a result, the triangle count for LOD2 meshes are very low, and they require a separate custom mesh and textures, which can be baked-in.

- The LOD2 version of the main mesh should just factor in the basic silhouette of the building as a whole.
- Triangle counts for LOD2 meshes are very low. For buildings, it should not exceed a maximum of 500 triangles.
- Minimum texture resolution for LOD2 is 512x512px. Any lower texture resolution is not supported.
- Maximum texture resolution for LOD2 is 1024x1024px.
- Alternatively, LOD2 mesh can share textures with main mesh, provided that your LOD2 UV layout is compatible with your main mesh texture, and you can reduce the triangle count below 500.
- If your asset features grass or water sub meshes, you can add a low-poly version of these sub meshes as it is supported.
- For the window sub meshes, it is acceptable to use a LOD2_Win sub mesh that is virtually the same as the main window sub mesh, as this allows you to have night time lighting on windows on the LOD2 zoom level.
While the triangle count of window sub meshes is usually relatively high, this is allowed as long as you have optimized your baked LOD2 mesh as much as possible, and only include polygons/windows on LOD2_Win that would actually be visible at distance. The polygons for the window sub mesh should sit floating above the baked LOD2 mesh, so they are not hidden by the simplified geometry of LOD2.LOD2 window meshes should have the exact same UV mapping as the main window sub mesh.

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
sub mesh should sit floating above the baked LOD2 mesh, so they are not hidden by the simplified geometry of LOD2.LOD2 window meshes should have the exact same UV mapping as the main window sub mesh.

- Creating the LOD2 version for these sub meshes depends on the visual identity of the building and use of the sub meshes. For some buildings it might be logical to ignore a small glass railing or strip of grass. The performance impact of sub meshes should be considered when deciding on their inclusion for LOD2, for example: removing the geometry representing the glass surface, and baking it down using a color similar to the tint of the glass would be appropriate.

### Mesh and Texture Export

- Main mesh must have one material assigned, having identical name as the mesh object/filename. Only one material per main mesh is supported.
- Sub meshes such as windows, grass and glass mustNOThave any material assigned.
- If your software doesn't allow the same name instance, then apply "_Mtl" at the end of the material name.
- Export main and sub meshes as 2018 version .fbx format.
- Mesh names and suffixes are case sensitive.

#### Example Export

title: Asset Pipeline: Buildings
text:
the same name instance, then apply "_Mtl" at the end of the material name.
- Export main and sub meshes as 2018 version .fbx format.
- Mesh names and suffixes are case sensitive.

title: Asset Pipeline: Decals
text:
# Asset Pipeline: Decals

Source: https://cs2.paradoxwikis.com/Asset_Pipeline:_Decals

Paradox logo black square Verified by Paradox Interactive
This article has been verified by Paradox Interactive for version 1.5.2 f1 of the game.

## Asset Design

AssetPipeline Decals

### Decorative Decals

Decorative Decals are usually non-functional, decorative decals that can be in various sizes, can be placed by the player in-game or used in the editor only.

### Functional Decals

These are decals that are used in conjunction with other mechanics. Decals such as parking lot decals are considered functional as they are used by vehicles. In practice however they are still decorative decals that have been then assigned to some functionality, so you only need to keep in mind any function you wish to give these decals when creating them, like the width and length of a vehicle for any parking decal.

### Size, Scale and Triangle Counts for Decals

- Decals can be virtually any size. Decals don’t have lot size parameters as they are considered ‘sub objects’. A decal uses its mesh boundary information to determine when it is rendered and its collision.
- The scale of decals should be 1:1, so if you wish to have a decal that is 2m in size, you need to model it to be 2m.
- Triangle count for decals should be very low. 12 triangles is a standard cube projection volume mesh.

## Asset Pipeline

title: Asset Pipeline: Decals
text:
f you wish to have a decal that is 2m in size, you need to model it to be 2m.
- Triangle count for decals should be very low. 12 triangles is a standard cube projection volume mesh.

## Asset Pipeline

A decal asset consists only of a main mesh, with no sub meshes, and generally no LOD meshes required.

Main Mesh The main mesh for a decal acts as the UV map of the projection of the decal, as well as the projection volume mesh. Projection volume refers to the 3D volume within which the decal is projected onto geometry.

LOD (Level of Detail) Meshes The main mesh of a decal should have very, very low triangle count. This means there is no need to make any LOD meshes.

### Main Mesh

#### Overview

Create a simple 2D shape that would suit the shape of your decal. Make sure the origin point of the mesh is located in the center of this mesh, and that the mesh is located at 0 on the vertical axis. Your UV mapping should be set to this shape, where from the top down view you can see your decal as it should be rendered in-game. Make sure the edges of the mesh are UV mapped to a transparent part of your decal, so they are rendered invisible.

Your 2D shape should now be extruded in both directions along the vertical axis. This creates a volumetric shape the game can use for the ‘projection volume’.

#### Modelling Conventions

title: Asset Pipeline: Decals
text:
d invisible.

Your 2D shape should now be extruded in both directions along the vertical axis. This creates a volumetric shape the game can use for the ‘projection volume’.

#### Modelling Conventions

Before starting the modelling process, the content creator should:

- Assess the size of the shape of the decal they wish to have projecting on other geometry. visual properties of the prop and decide which visual features are the most important to model, and/or represent them on the texture.
- Make sure the scale and dimensions of the props are 1:1.
- Object pivot is important to establish properly. The pivot location determines how the decal is placed, whether on the terrain or on buildings and other props in the editor.
- The simpler the shape of the decal the better, generally most decal meshes would be a cube or rectangular prism.
Note: Projection of a decal is always top down, regardless of any UV mapping on the sides of your main mesh. For decals to project sideways, will require rotating the mesh after it is imported.

#### UV Mapping of Main Mesh

- UV range has to be between 0 and 1 and spacing between UV islands should be large enough to avoid issues with mipmapping.
- The overall Decal UV coordinate extents are used to calculate and automatically assign the decal position on the imported projection volume. These values can be changed after import.

title: Asset Pipeline: Decals
text:
ith mipmapping.
- The overall Decal UV coordinate extents are used to calculate and automatically assign the decal position on the imported projection volume. These values can be changed after import.

#### Naming Convention for Main Mesh

- It is important to name your main mesh clearly, as it will be the bases for naming materials, textures and sub meshes.
- Mesh object names in the 3D software must be identical with the filename.

Filename Mesh Object

Main Mesh ExampleAsset.fbx ExampleAsset

### Textures

Decals in Cities: Skylines II uses a variation of the standard PBR pipeline (with metallic/glossiness), with custom additions in order to cover the game’s special needs.

In order to create a Cities: Skylines II compliant decal asset, you need to produce as a minimum the following texture types and import into the game:

BaseColor,
MaskMap,
Normal,

title: Asset Pipeline: Decals
text:
me’s special needs.

In order to create a Cities: Skylines II compliant decal asset, you need to produce as a minimum the following texture types and import into the game:

BaseColor,
MaskMap,
Normal,

- Due to the UV coordinate extents being used to calculate the decal position, it tested best with one decal per texture. The example decal “Paradox Mods Logo” uses the smallest size of 512x512.
- The resolution of textures should be no more than 4096x4096 pixels, and no less than 512x512 pixels. Best practice for decals is to use smaller resolution for smaller decals as it does not make sense to use 4096x4096 textures for a tiny decal. Best practice is to start at the smallest resolution and only increase if absolutely necessary.
- All textures must be .png and having identical resolutions.
- The game uses virtual texturing and will automatically downsize texture resolution based on the user's settings.

- Texture sharing works at its most optimised when textures are shared across assets that will generally be in view of the camera together and therefore rendered at the same time.

title: Asset Pipeline: Decals
text:
he user's settings.

- Texture sharing works at its most optimised when textures are shared across assets that will generally be in view of the camera together and therefore rendered at the same time.

- The art for the Base Game is specifically a "clean" visual style, with minimal grunge or aging effects.
- You can of course add weather and grunge/dirt to your custom assets. Make sure to consider the amount of grunge and aging, and that they will not cause repetitive patterns on the textures, especially on rooftops and walls.
- Due to the decal shader’s rendering properties, it is recommended to darken and desaturate your BaseColor as standard values used in other pipelines may look overexposed.

Depending on what software you use, you can create your own textures with the following channel structure to support decal creation:

BaseColor

Channel Channel Name Description

RGB BaseColor Diffuse Map The basic diffuse map for your mesh.

Alpha Opa city Defines which parts of the texture are alpha clipped. White is fully visible and Black is transparent. The alpha clip threshold is at 50% Black. It either renders as opaque or fully transparent.

Naming Convention: ExampleAsset_BaseColor.png

MaskMap

Channel Channel Name Description

title: Asset Pipeline: Decals
text:
ck is transparent. The alpha clip threshold is at 50% Black. It either renders as opaque or fully transparent.

Naming Convention: ExampleAsset_BaseColor.png

MaskMap

Channel Channel Name Description

R Metallic Defines how metallic the diffuse renders in the game. White is full metallic, Black is not metallic at all. Decals should generally leave this channel black.

G Coat Applies an extra shine, or glossy coat to the diffuse on top of the standard glossiness. White is a full coat, Black is no coat. Decals should generally leave this channel black.

B Default Black This channel is not currently used and should remain Black.

Alpha Glossiness Defines the glossiness of the diffuse. White is very glossy, Black is not glossy at all. In the standard PBR workflow, glossiness is the inverse of roughness.

Naming Convention: ExampleAsset_MaskMap.png

Normal

Channel Channel Name Description

title: Asset Pipeline: Decals
text:
is very glossy, Black is not glossy at all. In the standard PBR workflow, glossiness is the inverse of roughness.

Naming Convention: ExampleAsset_MaskMap.png

Normal

Channel Channel Name Description

RGB Normal Map Your normal map uses OpenGL. Normal maps are used to visually add depth to the diffuse textures. A decal can have normal mapped details, or you can have your normal map be flat using the RGB values of 128,128,255. Including the normal map is required to not break virtual textures - however in your import settings, you can set the decal to not use its normal map information. This can be good for creators wishing to add decals to walls and have the wall normal mapping show through decal. See more: Importing Decals

Naming Convention: ExampleAsset_Normal.png

#### Optional Textures

Optionally, you may find uses for the other textures but they are not necessary and can be excluded based on your intended design. These textures are not guaranteed to work as expected, if you opt to try, follow the guidelines for these textures in the Asset Pipeline: Prop’s page.

ControlMask,
Emissive

ControlMask Note: ControlMask and Color variations have not been tested for the purposes of this guide and may or may not work.

Channel Channel Name Description

title: Asset Pipeline: Decals
text:
: Prop’s page.

ControlMask,
Emissive

ControlMask Note: ControlMask and Color variations have not been tested for the purposes of this guide and may or may not work.

Channel Channel Name Description

R Color mask 1 Color masks come in sets of 3 and are used together. Each color mask should be mapped mutually exclusive to other color masks. Black through to White applies the selected colors to the diffuse map using multiply blending. Black is no color, and White is full color.

Where each color mask is applied to your asset, the game will use it to assign one of the 3 color variations once imported. See also: Assets: Setting Up Color Variations

G Color mask 2

B Color mask 3

Alpha Snow Remove Specifies on the mesh where automatically generated snow is removed. White is no snow, Black is full snow. Note: SnowRemove is not yet implemented, so any use of the SnowRemove channel is for futureproofing your asset. Snow remove was not tested on the example decal.

Naming Convention: ExampleAsset_ControlMask.png

Emissive
Note: Emissive has not been tested for the purposes of this guide and may or may not work.

title: Asset Pipeline: Decals
text:
. Snow remove was not tested on the example decal.

Naming Convention: ExampleAsset_ControlMask.png

Emissive
Note: Emissive has not been tested for the purposes of this guide and may or may not work.

The game supports two, mutually exclusive Emissive art pipelines, however decal creators should use the single emissive pipeline for their asset: Single Emissive This is where the game expects a single controllable emissive source, which can select a single purpose from a range of different uses. The most common use being ‘decorative light’.

Channel Channel Name Description

RGB Emissive Map Applies an emissive effect to the building, color and brightness is set through the RGB values on this map.

Naming Convention: ExampleAsset_Emissive.png

### Mesh and Texture Export

- Main mesh must have one material assigned, having identical name as the mesh object/filename. Only one material per main mesh is supported.
- If your software doesn't allow the same name instance, then apply "_Mtl" at the end of the material name.
- Export main and sub meshes as 2018 version .fbx format.
- Mesh names and suffixes are case sensitive.

#### Example Export

title: Asset Pipeline: Decals
text:
the same name instance, then apply "_Mtl" at the end of the material name.
- Export main and sub meshes as 2018 version .fbx format.
- Mesh names and suffixes are case sensitive.

#### Example Export

'ExampleAsset' is a shed having multiple emissive textures to light different parts of the shed with different colors. It has 1 window type, no glass, no grass and no water sub mesh. LOD1 is sharing textures with the main mesh and LOD2 is low poly mesh with baked custom textures. The naming convention and final export configuration would be the following:
