title: Asset Pipeline: Props
text:
# Asset Pipeline: Props

Source: https://cs2.paradoxwikis.com/Asset_Pipeline:_Props

Verified by Paradox Interactive
This article has been verified by Paradox Interactive for version 1.5.2 f1 of the game.

## Asset Design

AssetPipeline Props

### Static Props

Static Props are usually non-functional, decorative props that can be in various sizes, can be placed by the player in-game or used in the editor only. Industrial props such as smokestacks, toolsheds, barrels, nature props such as boulders, residential props and TV Antennas are considered as static props.

### Dynamic Props

These are props that are interacting with the simulation. Props such as benches which are used by citizens, traffic lights and railway barriers, any prop that gets spawned due to weather changes, brand or advertisement props that can change based on company occupancy of a building, are all considered dynamic props.

### Size, Scale and Triangle Counts for Props

title: Asset Pipeline: Props
text:
spawned due to weather changes, brand or advertisement props that can change based on company occupancy of a building, are all considered dynamic props.

### Size, Scale and Triangle Counts for Props

- Props can be virtually any size. Unlike buildings, props don’t have lot size parameters as they are considered ‘sub objects’. Instead a prop uses its mesh boundary information to determine when it is rendered and its collision.
- Scale of props should be 1:1. This especially applies for props that can be interacted with citizens, such as benches, tables, chairs or props and they can be used as part of an activity.
- Triangle count can vary based on the size and utility of the prop. The common sense approach applies here; you should model only what is logical to model.

## Asset Pipeline

A prop asset consists of a main mesh, any sub meshes and LOD meshes.

Main MeshIt consists of the basic visual elements of the prop. It acts as the parent mesh for all of the sub meshes associated with it.
Sub mesheshave to match the position, rotation and name of its parent mesh. Current collection of supported sub meshes and their required suffixes:

- Windows: Featuring parallax interior rooms with two versions.
- Glass: Clear transparent glass.
- Grass: Theme terrain based grass.
- Water: Moving water surface, semi transparent.

title: Asset Pipeline: Props
text:
quired suffixes:

- Windows: Featuring parallax interior rooms with two versions.
- Glass: Clear transparent glass.
- Grass: Theme terrain based grass.
- Water: Moving water surface, semi transparent.

LOD (Level of Detail) MeshesThese are lower poly versions of the main mesh. Cities: Skylines II props pipeline can have up to 2 LODs, varying in complexity and texture size for an efficient rendering performance. These meshes are called:

- LOD1: Simplified version of the main mesh, sharing the same texture.
- LOD2: Very low poly, baked-in mesh that gets triggered on a far distance.

Props must have LOD1, but LOD2 is optional and should be included depending on the size of the prop, and the expectation on whether it remains visible as your camera moves further away. If no LOD2 is provided, the prop will disappear at distance.

### Main Mesh

#### Overview

Before starting the modelling process, the content creator should:

title: Asset Pipeline: Props
text:
isible as your camera moves further away. If no LOD2 is provided, the prop will disappear at distance.

### Main Mesh

#### Overview

Before starting the modelling process, the content creator should:

- Assess the visual properties of the prop and decide which visual features are the most important to model, and/or represent them on the texture.
- Decide on the purpose of the prop. A static prop can be considered relatively simple to make, whereas a dynamic prop such as a traffic light, railway barrier or a clock will require additional, dedicated walkthroughs on extra pipeline information. This additional information will be added to the wiki later.
- Make sure the scale and dimensions of the props are 1:1.

#### Modelling Conventions

Props are as important as building modelisation - they assist the visual representation and identity of a building, creating its story. In Cities: Skylines II the amount of modelled details can be higher than in its predecessor without going too far over the top. Building windows for instance should be inset, window frames can be modelled if there are window frames and roof eaves should have interesting details. The same approach to level of detail also applies for props.

- Object pivot is important to establish properly. The pivot location determines how the prop is placed, whether on the terrain or on buildings and other props in the editor.

title: Asset Pipeline: Props
text:
also applies for props.

- Object pivot is important to establish properly. The pivot location determines how the prop is placed, whether on the terrain or on buildings and other props in the editor.

- The purpose of the prop dictates the pivot. A prop that is supposed to be used on uneven terrain should not have its pivot right at the bottom of the mesh, otherwise it may float. A prop that is expected to snap walls, should have some offset to ensure the snapping side is not floating over the wall, and the pivot should be at the point where the wall connection is made.

- The rotation of the prop is based on the pivot, just like buildings.

- Foundations are generated for props if they are used independently or placed freely on the lot terrain. Foundations will disable if the prop is parented to a building mesh.
- It is recommended to keep the topology as clean as possible and avoid any ghost vertices. This is important for props that can be snapped to each other just as ducts, vents and wall props.
Note: Rotating your mesh can lead to unintended issues for animations, best practice is to have the rotation of your mesh correct on import.

#### Tips on Optimisation

title: Asset Pipeline: Props
text:
as ducts, vents and wall props.
Note: Rotating your mesh can lead to unintended issues for animations, best practice is to have the rotation of your mesh correct on import.

#### Tips on Optimisation

While props can be great for a lot of detail in a small space, optimizing your model is still best practice. We recommend using normal mapping for details that are less than 5cm in depth (on average - some exceptions would be considered acceptable).

The "5cm" is not a hard rule. Depending on the size of the props and complexity, you might find yourself in a situation where optimisation will be necessary even for details larger than 5cm. This will be the part where as an asset creator you will have to prioritize the importance of the visual identity of the prop vs. the costs of labour and topology for performance impact. This is especially the case for props which the player is expected to investigate up close or first person level - you might want to create a table prop or an historical artefact with more details because they represent a higher visual value from the player’s perspective.

To keep less labour intensive during the production of the asset, below is the recommended list of areas you can optimize on the model:

title: Asset Pipeline: Props
text:
represent a higher visual value from the player’s perspective.

To keep less labour intensive during the production of the asset, below is the recommended list of areas you can optimize on the model:

- You can intersect meshes without creating additional topology. An example would be a steel beam being connected to a façade. You can just move the beam further inside the model instead of joining two topologies together.
- A door handle will be around 5cm extruded from a door surface and therefore should be modelled. Any other detail such as keyhole should be on the normal map if they are to be featured.
- The panels of a door may be 5cm deep, therefore they would be modelled, if however they are less than 5cm then a normal map may suffice.
- The average door frame could be 5cm (compared to the window glass surface) and would be modelled.
- The silhouette should be taken into account when considering detail - most details under 5cm depth do not change the silhouette of the model. If you would notice a distinct change in the silhouette then the detail should be modelled.
- Exceptions may include thin fencing - where you may alpha map the majority of the fence and model only the support framework to create visual depth.
- Things like ornamented façade details (like carved motifs or statuettes) can use a combination of modelling and normal mapping techniques to convey the details.
- Angle based auto-smooth is a great tool to take advantage of in any modelling software, it can allow you to model curved objects at lower costs of triangle count. You should aim for at least 60 degrees of auto smoothing, helping you to optimize curved details such as rain gutters, circular door handles or columns.
- Props with animations should be modeled with industry standard rigging standards in mind. Animated bodies such as clock handles, sails should not be welded with the static part of the mesh.

title: Asset Pipeline: Props
text:
columns.
- Props with animations should be modeled with industry standard rigging standards in mind. Animated bodies such as clock handles, sails should not be welded with the static part of the mesh.

#### UV Mapping of Main Mesh

- UV range has to be between 0 and 1 and spacing between UV islands should be large enough to avoid issues with mipmapping.
- The space needs to be even larger on the islands where mirrored UV's are used.
- Overlapping UV maps should be used whenever it is possible to save texture space. UV maps should be always laid out so that different materials are separated with empty space to avoid any bleed across UV islands. It is recommended to cluster similarly colored materials together to prevent bleeding between very dark and very light materials. If the contrast of color is too large, it is best to increase the space between two islands to prevent bleeding. Depending on your texture workflow, you can stack uv islands which should help keeping a higher texel density across the board, provided that textures do not feature any repetitive visual elements.

#### Naming Convention for Main Mesh

- It is important to name your main mesh clearly, as it will be the bases for naming materials, textures and sub meshes.
- Mesh object names in the 3D software must be identical with the filename.

FilenameMesh Object

Main MeshExampleAsset.fbxExampleAsset

title: Asset Pipeline: Props
text:

### Sub Meshes

Windows, glass surfaces, terrain and water surfaces that use special shaders in the game need to be modelled, UV-mapped and exported separately with an identical pivot to the main mesh. UV-mapping in most of the sub meshes can be done via auto UV-map on the modelling software of choice, with minimum amount of distortion. In some cases -such as windows- the orientation of UV islands matter. Sub meshes rely on shaders so there is no need for asset creators to create additional sets of textures for sub meshes.

Props share the identical pipeline with buildings regarding submeshes:Windows Sub Meshes

There are currently two window sub mesh variants and shaders, offering parallax based interior/room generation:

- Windows with opaque windows with interior map) =_Win
- Windows with opaque milk windows with blurred interior map) =_Wim
Note: While technically recognized by the game, the _Wim sub mesh parallax interior is currently identical to _Win.

Cities: Skylines II references the UV Map of windows sub meshes to generate parallax rooms as well as night lighting. The mapping and the distribution of windows directly influences the shader.

title: Asset Pipeline: Props
text:
_Win.

Cities: Skylines II references the UV Map of windows sub meshes to generate parallax rooms as well as night lighting. The mapping and the distribution of windows directly influences the shader.

The game currently supports 25 variations of rooms per UV Map. These rooms can feature different curtain types and alignments as well as light on/off states that are randomized based on building occupancy (except 2 variations that are simply on/off all times). Parallax interiors are assigned based on checksum on mesh names. The editor will assign different interiors based on the following words if they are included in your mesh name:

- Industrial
- Office
- ResidentialHigh
- ResidentialLow
- CommercialHigh
- CommercialLow

If the mesh name is custom, it will be using an office variant.

Modelling Windows Sub Meshes
The process of modelling windows is relatively straightforward:

title: Asset Pipeline: Props
text:
ntialLow
- CommercialHigh
- CommercialLow

If the mesh name is custom, it will be using an office variant.

Modelling Windows Sub Meshes
The process of modelling windows is relatively straightforward:

- Windows that use the interior mapping shader should be modelled separately from the main mesh.
- Don't use a single polygon behind several windows, Separate them as shown to the right. A single polygon behind a window that has several frames is okay.
- Make sure the window sub mesh is properly aligned with the window frames on the main mesh. You can use snapping options in the 3D software of your choice to align edges of the window faces to the frames. Alternatively, you can scale up windows a little to ensure windows sub mesh is well covering the empty space right behind the frames.

UV Mapping of Windows Sub Meshes

In order to make UV mapping convenient for asset creators, we provide a reference texture which will help you with windows UV mapping. While the game does not directly use this reference texture, it calculates night time illumination and interior room generation based on the location of UV islands on the UV coordinates.

There are 25 different color values in the texture:

title: Asset Pipeline: Props
text:
is reference texture, it calculates night time illumination and interior room generation based on the location of UV islands on the UV coordinates.

There are 25 different color values in the texture:

- Always on = When building is functional, these windows will always be lit at night
- Room 01-23 = When the building is functional, these windows will get randomized if they are lit or not at night.
- Always off = These windows will never be lit, even when the building is functional.

Asset creators can control the interior parallax "rooms" in the asset by UV-mapping different windows to different color value areas on the reference texture. Window UV's should always be placed logically to give the impression of rooms inside the asset.

UV Mapping Guidelines for Windows Sub Meshes

title: Asset Pipeline: Props
text:
s to different color value areas on the reference texture. Window UV's should always be placed logically to give the impression of rooms inside the asset.

UV Mapping Guidelines for Windows Sub Meshes

- Asset UV's should always use as many rooms as logically possible to give more variation and control over the asset.
- UV islands can be placed over the red marker text in the reference texture.
- The UV island orientation of windows needs to always match the world orientation of the corresponding polygon where the top edge of the window polygon is up in UV space.
- Overlapping UV's for windows are forbidden.
- Size of the window UV islands doesn't matter (but using maximum space available is still recommended).
- UV island correct aspect ratio should be respected - do not squish or stretch your windows, but scale them down if necessary.

When the prop is imported to the game, the final room layout will be created using this UV mapping as a starting point. If two windows that are in close logical proximity to each other in 3D space, and are mapped to the same room on the reference texture the game will calculate them as looking into the same single interior space.

Naming Convention for Windows Sub Meshes

- Windows sub meshes need to be named after the main mesh and they need to have a suffix designating their shaders.
- Mesh names and suffixes are case sensitive.

Glass Shader is used to imitate glass surfaces. Similar to window parallax, it is handled by the game and the asset creator doesn't need to provide any texture.

Modelling & UV Mapping Guidelines for Glass Sub Mesh

- Glass sub meshes should be modelled separately from the main mesh.
- Best practice for modelling glass would be to add some thickness - in other words, avoid using single planes for glass sub meshes. The glass shader is intended to be single sided and will not render on back faces.
- As for the UV mapping, glass sub mesh can be auto UV mapped. The current shader features some smears and dirt on the glass; if you have a large glass canopy made of out polygons, it is recommended to UV map manually instead.
- Overlapping UV islands is forbidden.

Naming Convention for Glass Sub Mesh

- Overlapping UV islands is forbidden.

Naming Convention for Glass Sub Mesh

- Glass sub mesh needs to be named after the main mesh and it needs to have a suffix designating its shader.
- Mesh names and suffixes are case sensitive.

AssetPipeline CastleGrass

Grass shader is available for buildings and props to use terrain texture on buildings and props. The sub mesh acts like a continuation of the terrain texture. The mesh will sample the terrain texture on the terrain below it and project it up onto the surface of the sub mesh.

Modelling & UV Mapping Guidelines for Grass Sub Mesh

- UV unwrap with minimal distortion.
- Overlapping UV islands is forbidden.
- Auto UV unwrap can be used, as this shader is projecting the terrain texture.

Naming Convention for Grass Sub Mesh

- Grass sub mesh needs to be named after the main mesh and it needs to have a suffix designating its shader.
- Mesh names and suffixes are case sensitive.

AssetPipeline Pool

The water shader is used to create pools or fountains. The shader is semi-transparent, and has a loop animation to mimic the water surface.

Modelling & UV Mapping Guidelines for Water Sub Mesh

- UV unwrap with minimal distortion.
- Overlapping UV islands is forbidden.
- Consider maximizing the UV space as the scale of the water texture depends on the size of the UV islands.
- If you have a polygonal surface for water, consider manual UV unwrapping instead and do not split the uv topology.
- As this is a semi-transparent shader, you must model any surface on the main mesh which will be rendered under this sub mesh.
- If the water sub mesh is enclosed with another sub mesh using the glass shader, water will not be rendered.

Naming Convention for Water Sub Mesh

- Water sub mesh needs to be named after the main mesh and it needs to have a suffix designating its shader.
- Mesh names and suffixes are case sensitive.

### Textures

Props in Cities: Skylines II uses a variation of the standard PBR pipeline (with metallic/glossiness), with custom additions in order to cover the game’s special needs.

In order to create a Cities: Skylines II compliant prop asset, you need to produce the following texture types and import into the game:

BaseColor,
ControlMask,
MaskMap,
Normal,
Emissive

- The resolution of textures should be no more than 4096x4096 pixels, and no less than 512x512 pixels. Best practice for props is to use smaller resolution for smaller props as it does not make sense to use 4096x4096 textures for a tiny prop.
- All textures must be .png and having identical resolutions.
- The game uses virtual texturing and will automatically downsize texture resolution based on the user's settings.
- If you have multiple props which can share components and elements, you can texture them together with a single set of textures. This is called texture sharing and how to import assets sharing textures is explained in the Importing section.

title: Asset Pipeline: Props
text:
re components and elements, you can texture them together with a single set of textures. This is called texture sharing and how to import assets sharing textures is explained in the Importing section.

- Texture sharing works at its most optimised when textures are shared across assets that will generally be in view of the camera together and therefore rendered at the same time.

- SnowRemove (alpha channel on ControlMask) will be used to control snow accumulation on the surface of the asset. Mapping UV islands to distinguish between snow and SnowRemove should be considered. SnowRemove should be applied to surfaces like terraces, stairs and balconies and any areas that have roofing over them. Applying SnowRemove to a UV island 'removes' or forbids snow accumulation.
Note: SnowRemove is not yet implemented, so any use of the SnowRemove channel is for futureproofing your asset.

- The art for the Base Game is specifically a "clean" visual style, with minimal grunge or aging effects.
- You can of course add weather and grunge/dirt to your custom assets. Make sure to consider the amount of grunge and aging, and that they will not cause repetitive patterns on the textures, especially on rooftops and walls.

Depending on what software you use, you can create your own textures with the following channel structure to support prop creation:

BaseColor

ChannelChannel NameDescription

Depending on what software you use, you can create your own textures with the following channel structure to support prop creation:

BaseColor

ChannelChannel NameDescription

RGBBaseColor Diffuse MapThe basic diffuse map for your mesh.

AlphaOpa cityDefines which parts of the texture are alpha clipped. White is fully visible and Black is transparent. The alpha clip threshold is at 50% Black. It either renders as opaque or fully transparent.

Naming Convention:ExampleAsset_BaseColor.png

ControlMask

ChannelChannel NameDescription

RColor mask 1Color masks come in sets of 3 and are used together. Each color mask should be mapped mutually exclusive to other color masks. Black through to White applies the selected colors to the diffuse map using multiply blending. Black is no color, and White is full color.

Where each color mask is applied to your asset, the game will use it to assign one of the 3 color variations once imported. See also:Assets: Setting Up Color Variations

GColor mask 2

BColor mask 3

AlphaSnow RemoveSpecifies on the mesh where automatically generated snow is removed. White is no snow, Black is full snow. Note: SnowRemove is not yet implemented, so any use of the SnowRemove channel is for futureproofing your asset.

Naming Convention:ExampleAsset_ControlMask.png

MaskMap

ChannelChannel NameDescription

title: Asset Pipeline: Props
text:
Note: SnowRemove is not yet implemented, so any use of the SnowRemove channel is for futureproofing your asset.

Naming Convention:ExampleAsset_ControlMask.png

MaskMap

ChannelChannel NameDescription

RMetallicDefines how metallic the diffuse renders in the game. White is full metallic, Black is not metallic at all.

GCoatApplies an extra shine, or glossy coat to the diffuse on top of the standard glossiness. White is a full coat, Black is no coat.

BDefault BlackThis channel is not currently used and should remain Black.

AlphaGlossinessDefines the glossiness of the diffuse. White is very glossy, Black is not glossy at all. In the standard PBR workflow, glossiness is the inverse of roughness.

Naming Convention:ExampleAsset_MaskMap.png

Normal

ChannelChannel NameDescription

RGBNormal MapYour normal map uses OpenGL. Normal maps are used to visually add depth to the diffuse textures.

Naming Convention:ExampleAsset_Normal.png

Emissive
The game supports two, mutually exclusive Emissive art pipelines, creators should choose the most appropriate for their asset:

- Single Emissive sources
- Multiple Emissive sources

Single EmissiveThis is where the game expects a single controllable emissive source, which can select a single purpose from a range of different uses. The most common use being ‘decorative light’.

ChannelChannel NameDescription

title: Asset Pipeline: Props
text:
the game expects a single controllable emissive source, which can select a single purpose from a range of different uses. The most common use being ‘decorative light’.

ChannelChannel NameDescription

RGBEmissive MapApplies an emissive effect to the building, color and brightness is set through the RGB values on this map.

Naming Convention:ExampleAsset_Emissive.png

Multiple EmissiveThis is where the game allows advanced control over multiple emissive sources. This is made functional with the combined use of Emissive textures and Emissive ID textures. Note: When UV mapping the Multiple Emissive textures, there can be a maximum of 4 lights per triangles, or triangle neighbours if the vertices are shared across multiple triangles that get light contribution so be cautious when authoring emissive or tessellate the model accordingly. As a rule of thumb, more than 4 lights on any one UV island will likely cause problems.

An Emissive texture and Emissive ID texture work together as a pair, where the game can recognize up to 4 pairs of Emissive and Emissive ID textures. The textures are named and paired as follows:

Emissive TextureEmissive ID Texture

Emissive Textures work the same way as the Single Emissive texture:

ChannelChannel NameDescription

RGBEmissive MapApplies an emissive effect to the building, color and brightness is set through the RGB values on this map.

Emissive ID Textures are used to encode a light ID from 1 to 255. For convenience, you can choose whatever value you wish, for example every tenth, so that you can tell them apart more easily. Using two values that are right next to each other is not recommended since they seem to become merged into one in some cases. 0 is always no light.

ChannelChannel NameDescription

GrayscaleEmissive ID MapPixel-perfect masking of different light sources, or light IDs. If a pixel is set to 10 it will be assigned a light ID different to a pixel set to 20. By assigning pixels that are the same coordinates to the Emissive texture the same gray value on the Texture ID will result in the game treating those pixels as the same light source, and therefore control the emissive of those pixels together. Note:

text:
to the Emissive texture the same gray value on the Texture ID will result in the game treating those pixels as the same light source, and therefore control the emissive of those pixels together. Note:

- If multiple lights overlap the same pixel areas, then another emissive map is needed, up to 4 maps. Even when using a single map, the maximum of 4 lights per triangle applies.
- Emissive Textures may use infinite dilation, but Emissive ID textures must not, and must be pixel-perfect, no anti-aliasing or blending to 0.

### LOD Meshes

LOD meshes are lower poly representations of the original mesh topology. They are triggered when the camera moves away from the prop location. The threshold to trigger LOD meshes depends on the user graphics settings, size of the mesh.

Props should always have LOD1. For props that should be visible when further zoomed out, they should also have LOD2.

LOD1 Mesh and Textures
LOD1 is the intermediate LOD between main mesh and far LOD2. This is usually the mesh which most of the players will see from the usual distance city-builder games are being played. Because of this, it should be made out of main mesh, however it should feature less details and mesh complexity.

title: Asset Pipeline: Props
text:
st of the players will see from the usual distance city-builder games are being played. Because of this, it should be made out of main mesh, however it should feature less details and mesh complexity.

- The triangle count should be less than 50% of the main mesh’s triangle count.
- Small mesh features such as door handles, ornaments, rooftop details, pipes, rain gutters can be manually removed from LOD1 mesh.
- Window and door frames should be simplified while retaining the overall topology.
- For a quick result, you can use a decimate modifier of the 3D software of your choice and reduce the polycount via the software algorithm.
Note: If you are using any modifier to simplify your main mesh, make sure the UV mapping is not being severely altered in the process. Be sure to keep as much of the original silhouette as possible to avoid popping as the LOD level changes.

- LOD1 Mesh shares textures with the main mesh.
- LOD1 main mesh requires LOD1 sub meshes as well. The process explained above applies to sub meshes except windows..
- UV mapping of windows sub meshes defines parallax shader features, so you should avoid simplifying the topology.
Note: When simplifying the main mesh into LOD1, take care around the areas the _Win sub mesh should appear, you should avoid topology that could create gaps or otherwise cover up the sub mesh. See also:Texturing Sharing

title: Asset Pipeline: Props
text:
ing the main mesh into LOD1, take care around the areas the _Win sub mesh should appear, you should avoid topology that could create gaps or otherwise cover up the sub mesh. See also:Texturing Sharing

LOD2 Mesh and Textures
LOD2, is the far LOD. It gets triggered when the distance between camera and prop is very high, and just rendering its silhouette is enough. As a result, the triangle count for LOD2 meshes are very low, and they require a separate custom mesh and textures, which can be baked-in.

- The LOD2 version of the main mesh should just factor in the basic silhouette of the prop as a whole.
- Triangle counts for LOD2 meshes are very low. For most of the props, the overall LOD2 topology should be very basic, not requiring any high triangle count. For very large props, LOD2 triangle count should not exceed a maximum of 500 triangles.

- This means if the largest prop should only have up to 500 triangles, the smallest prop could be as little as 10 to really be optimised.

title: Asset Pipeline: Props
text:
triangle count should not exceed a maximum of 500 triangles.

- This means if the largest prop should only have up to 500 triangles, the smallest prop could be as little as 10 to really be optimised.

- Minimum texture resolution for LOD2 is 512x512px. Any lower texture resolution is not supported.
- Maximum texture resolution for LOD2 is 1024x1024px.
- Alternatively, LOD2 mesh can share textures with main mesh, provided that your LOD2 UV layout is compatible with your main mesh texture.
- If your asset features grass or water sub meshes, you can add a low-poly version of these sub meshes as it is supported.
- While it is possible to use LOD2 for windows sub mesh, they should ideally be baked-in or included in the LOD2 mesh.
- If your prop must use window sub meshes because it has windows large enough, or visible enough to be seen at that distance, it is acceptable to use a LOD2_Win sub mesh that is virtually the same as the main window sub mesh, as this allows you to have night time lighting on windows on the LOD2 zoom level.
While the triangle count of window sub meshes is usually relatively high, this is allowed as long as you have optimized your baked LOD2 mesh as much as possible, and only include polygons/windows on LOD2_Win that would actually be visible at distance. The polygons for the window sub mesh should sit floating above the baked LOD2 mesh, so they are not hidden by the simplified geometry of LOD2.LOD2 window meshes should have the exact same UV mapping as the main window sub mesh.

title: Asset Pipeline: Props
text:
sub mesh should sit floating above the baked LOD2 mesh, so they are not hidden by the simplified geometry of LOD2.LOD2 window meshes should have the exact same UV mapping as the main window sub mesh.

- Creating the LOD2 version for these sub meshes depends on the visual identity of the prop and use of the sub meshes. For some props it might be logical to ignore a small glass railing or strip of grass. The performance impact of sub meshes should be considered when deciding on their inclusion for LOD2, for example: removing the geometry representing the glass surface, and baking it down using a color similar to the tint of the glass would be appropriate.

### Mesh and Texture Export

- Main mesh must have one material assigned, having identical name as the mesh object/filename. Only one material per main mesh is supported.
- Sub meshes such as windows, grass and glass mustNOThave any material assigned.
- If your software doesn't allow the same name instance, then apply "_Mtl" at the end of the material name.
- Export main and sub meshes as 2018 version .fbx format.
- Mesh names and suffixes are case sensitive.

title: Asset Pipeline: Surfaces
text:
# Asset Pipeline: Surfaces

Source: https://cs2.paradoxwikis.com/Asset_Pipeline:_Surfaces

Paradox logo black square Verified by Paradox Interactive
This article has been verified by Paradox Interactive for version 1.5.6 f1 of the game.

## Asset Design

### Decorative Surfaces

Decorative Surfaces change the visual appearance of the terrain within their drawn boundary. Examples include grass, concrete, gravel, or sand overlays. They have no gameplay function beyond their appearance.

### Functional Surfaces

Functional Surfaces are tied to specific game mechanics or zone types. Agriculture, Forestry, Aquaculture, and Landfill surfaces are examples — the surface defines both the visual ground material and signals to the game systems that a particular activity takes place in that area. When creating a functional surface, keep in mind any size, shape, or visual constraints imposed by the mechanic it supports.

### Size and Shape

- Surfaces have no fixed size — players draw them as freehand polygons of any shape.
- Surfaces support very fine node spacing, down to 0.75m between boundary points, allowing highly detailed and organic shapes.
- The surface geometry is triangulated automatically and updates dynamically when underlying road nodes move through the surface area.

## Asset Pipeline

title: Asset Pipeline: Surfaces
text:
, allowing highly detailed and organic shapes.
- The surface geometry is triangulated automatically and updates dynamically when underlying road nodes move through the surface area.

## Asset Pipeline

A Surface asset consists entirely of a prefab and its three textures. There is no mesh to model or export.

Textures
Three textures are required to define the visual appearance of a textured surface. Surfaces that use only a flat colour overlay do not require textures.

Prefab
The prefab holds all configuration for the surface, including texture references, edge blending, render priority, and UI settings.

### Textures

Surfaces in Cities: Skylines II use a custom decal-based shader pipeline. The MaskMap channel layout for surfaces differs from the standard building shader — refer carefully to the channel definitions below.

In order to create a Cities: Skylines II compliant surface asset, it is recommended to produce the following texture types and import them into the game:

BaseColor,
MaskMap,
Normal,
Note: If you don't import one or all of the textures, it may work now, however in the future if surfaces are updated to use Virtual Textures, the custom surface may need updating to include the missing textures for proper texture atlassing.

title: Asset Pipeline: Surfaces
text:
the textures, it may work now, however in the future if surfaces are updated to use Virtual Textures, the custom surface may need updating to include the missing textures for proper texture atlassing.

- All textures must be .png with identical resolutions to each other.
- Texture resolution must be a power of two: 512x512, 1024x1024, or 2048x2048 pixels.
- 1024x1024 is recommended for most surfaces. Only use 2048x2048 if fine close-up detail is required.
- As the textures of a surface are repeating infinitely, Virtual Texturing is not applied to them, keep this is mind when choosing how large the textures are.
- All three textures must tile seamlessly, as they repeat across the drawn surface area controlled by the UV Scale setting.
- The art for the Base Game is a clean visual style with minimal grunge or aging effects. You may add weathering to custom assets, but avoid patterns that cause visible repetition when tiled.

BaseColor

Channel Channel Name Description

RGB BaseColor Diffuse Map The diffuse colour of your ground surface. Do not bake in any lighting, shadows or ambient occlusion — the engine handles all lighting at runtime.

Alpha Opa city The alpha channel of the BaseColor texture is used to control surface transparency, on a per-pixel basis.

Naming Convention: ExampleSurface_BaseColor.png

title: Asset Pipeline: Surfaces
text:
dles all lighting at runtime.

Alpha Opa city The alpha channel of the BaseColor texture is used to control surface transparency, on a per-pixel basis.

Naming Convention: ExampleSurface_BaseColor.png

MaskMap Note: The Surface shader uses a custom channel layout. This is different from the standard building shader MaskMap. Do not use building shader channel references for surfaces.

Channel Channel Name Description

R Metallic Defines how metallic the surface renders. White is fully metallic, Black is not metallic at all. Most natural ground surfaces should leave this channel black. Only push toward white for metallic or very wet surfaces.

G MetallicMask A mask to control where the Metallic (R) and Smoothness (A) values are applied. White applies those properties at full strength. Black causes the surface to ignore its own metallic and smoothness values entirely, allowing the terrain beneath to show through. For most surfaces, keep this channel white.

B NormalMask A mask controlling where the Normal Map is applied. White applies the normal map at full strength. Black causes the normal map to be ignored, allowing the terrain's underlying normals to show through instead. For most surfaces, keep this channel white.

title: Asset Pipeline: Surfaces
text:
White applies the normal map at full strength. Black causes the normal map to be ignored, allowing the terrain's underlying normals to show through instead. For most surfaces, keep this channel white.

Alpha Glossiness Defines the glossiness of the surface. White is very glossy, Black is not glossy at all. Dry natural surfaces such as grass or dirt should use near-black values. Hard surfaces such as concrete or pavement use a mid-grey value.

Naming Convention: ExampleSurface_MaskMap.png

Normal

Channel Channel Name Description

RGB Normal Map A tangent-space normal map using OpenGL conventions (blue-purple). Used to add perceived surface detail and depth to the ground material. Must be exported from your software in linear colour space, not as a standard colour image. A flat normal map can be represented with RGB values of 128, 128, 255.

Naming Convention: ExampleSurface_Normal.png

### Texture Reference Values

The following values from the game's own surface assets are provided as a starting reference:

Surface Type R Metallic G MetallicMask B DetailMask A Smoothness

Concrete Black (0) White (1) White (1) Mid grey (~0.5)

Grass / Agriculture Near black (~0.1) White (1) White (1) Near black (~0.04)

# Assets: Common Asset Principles

Source: https://cs2.paradoxwikis.com/Assets:_Common_Asset_Principles

Verified by Paradox Interactive
This article has been verified by Paradox Interactive for version 1.5.2 f1 of the game.

## Component

Props and other objects in Cities: Skylines II are separated into different categories based on their use and function.
Any object-like asset that is placed on another is considered a ‘sub object’ and can therefore be found in theObject Sub Objectscomponent on the asset you’re decorating.
Objects such as light fixtures, trash bins, benches, markers and placeholders are all included underObject Sub Objectcomponent once they are bound onto an asset.

### Common Props Types

title: Assets: Common Asset Principles
text:
re decorating.
Objects such as light fixtures, trash bins, benches, markers and placeholders are all included underObject Sub Objectcomponent once they are bound onto an asset.

### Common Props Types

- Regular Props:- Decorative only items such as flowers, plants, AC Units, vents and so on fall into this category. These types of props require no interaction from the pedestrian AI and as such they do not require access to walking areas, lanes or networks.
- Dynamic Props:- Benches, chairs, sundecks, tables are part of dynamic props that will be interacted with by citizens. These props, per function, will require an area or network that has a pedestrian lane so they can be connected to the rest of the pedestrian routing in the building.
- Light Props:- Light props are functional items that also include a physical light effect that can be turned on/off depending on the in-game day/night cycle. Light props are placed just like the normal props.
Note: Light props should be used carefully and not excessively. Too many lights in a small space will cause rendering artifacts.

### Placeholder and Random Props

Assets CommonPrinciplesRandomProps

Placeholder and Random props work as an identifier. They are visually represented by a placeholder mesh and checker textures once they are placed inside a building lot.

title: Assets: Common Asset Principles
text:
ts CommonPrinciplesRandomProps

Placeholder and Random props work as an identifier. They are visually represented by a placeholder mesh and checker textures once they are placed inside a building lot.

- Placeholder Props:- These are identifier props that change the mesh of the prop based on specific conditions. Placeholder props are commonly used on networks or assets where player interaction influences the visual representation of the prop. For example: lane direction markers, road signage, and roadside tree placement have placeholder props that allow the game to swap these with different props depending on the situation.
- Random Props:- If you want some variety in the prop style and prefer it to be randomized, you can use randomised props. You can find these props in their respective sections in the asset browser, or simply by searching the term ‘Random’.

title: Assets: Common Asset Principles
text:
ty in the prop style and prefer it to be randomized, you can use randomised props. You can find these props in their respective sections in the asset browser, or simply by searching the term ‘Random’.

When your asset is placed or spawned in-game the Random or Placeholder prop will choose from a pool of assets that have it listed in theirSpawnable Objectcomponent, creating random variation for that sub object. The probability of the sub object being spawned is based on the probability setting in each respective asset’sSpawnable Objectcomponent.
If you would like to randomize an entire group of props and prefer them to switch styles together, they should be under a shared group index, preferably a unique group index for these props.

### Using Props

title: Assets: Common Asset Principles
text:
would like to randomize an entire group of props and prefer them to switch styles together, they should be under a shared group index, preferably a unique group index for these props.

### Using Props

- Once you’ve selected an appropriate asset, make sure the “Binds overlapping items to a building” snapping option is toggled on.
- Placing a sub object onto a host asset is as simple as ‘point and click’.
- The mouse uses raycasting to determine the target location. Make sure you have a direct line of sight for where you would like to place your sub object.
- Raycasting below ground requires a clear line of sight. Terrain, meshes and other sub objects may obstruct the raycasting, so using Clip Surfaces and the underground view will allow you a clear line of sight.
- Investigate the differentSnapping optionsyou can use.
- Fine tune any placed prop by clicking on it and using the Sub Object Parameters as detailed below.

### Markers and Spawners

Assets CommonPrinciplesMarkers02

Markers and Spawners are functional props that are used to route pathfinding inside an asset, or spawn other assets (like animals).
With the exception of animal spawners, markers and spawners cannot work alone as they need access to physical spaces (such as roads, lanes, areas) to spawn pedestrians or vehicles.

There are multiple spawner and markers for pedestrians use:

title: Assets: Common Asset Principles
text:
rkers and spawners cannot work alone as they need access to physical spaces (such as roads, lanes, areas) to spawn pedestrians or vehicles.

There are multiple spawner and markers for pedestrians use:

- Pedestrian Access Location:- These are markers used to connect two adjacent areas or an area with a path/road, allowing pedestrian flow. These markers are also used to control the flow of pedestrian AI and point where they should enter the building lot.
- Pedestrian Spawn Location:- These are markers that represent ‘doors’ for pedestrians. They can be used by pedestrians to enter or exit a building, or to teleport between other markers that are considered a door, whether another Pedestrian Spawn Location or a Restricted Spawn Location.
- Restricted Spawn Location:- These are markers that represent private or restricted ‘doors’ for pedestrians. They can only be used by pedestrians that live on the asset, or are employed on the asset. As long as they respect those restrictions, pedestrians can use them to enter or exit a building, or to teleport between other markers that are considered a door, whether a Pedestrian Spawn Location or another Restricted Spawn Location.

There are also spawn markers for vehicles.

title: Assets: Common Asset Principles
text:
exit a building, or to teleport between other markers that are considered a door, whether a Pedestrian Spawn Location or another Restricted Spawn Location.

There are also spawn markers for vehicles.

- Car Spawn Location:- Used to spawn wheeled vehicles from depots. Requires an invisible road or vehicle path connection.
- Car Garage Location:- Designated marker to spawn or despawn parked vehicles inside a garage.
- Tram-,Train-, orSubway Spawn Location:- Used on their respective depot building. They require a physical tram, train or subway track to spawn vehicles.
- Helicopter Spawn Location:- Used to designate the landing pad for helicopters. Helicopter spawn location does not require helipad decorations; you can create your own custom helipad decoration or integrate the helipad into the building mesh. However you need to designate the radius of the restricted airspace around the pad. Helipad Airspacemarkers come in three different sizes. Use whichever is more convenient and realistic.
Note: The game will not spawn any building colliding with the airspace.

### Using Markers

title: Assets: Common Asset Principles
text:
e pad. Helipad Airspacemarkers come in three different sizes. Use whichever is more convenient and realistic.
Note: The game will not spawn any building colliding with the airspace.

### Using Markers

- Once you’ve selected a marker, make sure the “Binds overlapping items to a building” snapping option is toggled on.
- All markers have an effective radius to attach to nearby networks, lanes, props and areas.
- Pedestrian Access Locations are used to connect two adjacent areas, or one area to one lane/road.
- Pedestrian-, and Restricted Spawn Locations should be placed further inside the mesh, usually behind a modelled door. This will prevent them from being spawned or despawned visibly to the camera.
- You should avoid placing spawners and door markers too close to the edge of the lot. The game may re-route pathfinding between markers of two different buildings if the path is shorter.
- If you have two large adjacent areas that are supposed to be connected, consider placing multiple markers along the border to provide multiple transition paths for pedestrians.
- Vehicle spawners should be placed further inside the building mesh, usually behind a modelled garage door, farther enough for the vehicle that is expected to be spawned to be hidden from view as it does. For longer vehicles such as fire trucks, buses, trains and trams, consider creating a network layout that reaches deep inside the building and then place the spawners in those locations. You can check the base game fire station and train depot as a reference.
- You can place markers underground. Switch to the underground view and place the markers. Any marker that is underground will not be interactable in above ground mode.
- Avoid placing any vehicle spawn markers too close to the edge of the lot, even if they are properly connected to the in-lot paths and networks. The game may override in-lot routing and directly spawn vehicles to a road adjacent to the building instead. You can test the accessibility by using the gizmo tool.

title: Assets: Common Asset Principles
text:
d to the in-lot paths and networks. The game may override in-lot routing and directly spawn vehicles to a road adjacent to the building instead. You can test the accessibility by using the gizmo tool.

## Sub Objects Parameters

Assets CommonPrinciplesSubObjects

In order to edit any sub object properties you can either browse the list on the Object Sub Object component, or simply select the sub object placed on the asset. Either option will provide identical Sub Object Parameters to edit. For sub objects (including trees) you have the following parameters:

- Object:- Lists the selected sub object in a dropdown. You can click on this dropdown list and swap the prop with another object.
- Position:- Registers the current coordinates of the sub object with respect to the center of the lot. Y is the world vertical axis. X is the width axis and Z is the depth axis. Editing these values will update the sub object’s position.
- Rotation:- Registers the current rotation of the sub object with respect to the lot. Editing these values will update the sub object’s angles of rotation.
- Parent Mesh:- Registers which mesh the prop is being attached to.

title: Assets: Common Asset Principles
text:
he current rotation of the sub object with respect to the lot. Editing these values will update the sub object’s angles of rotation.
- Parent Mesh:- Registers which mesh the prop is being attached to.

- Parent Mesh -1 is always the world terrain, any sub object assigned this will attempt to match the height of the terrain the host asset is placed or spawned on.
 - Parent Mesh 0 is the mesh listed as ‘element 0’ in the meshes list of the host asset.
 - Parent Mesh 1 and so on is the mesh listed as ‘element 1’ in the meshes list of the host asset.
 - Parent Mesh 1000 and above show when you have parented a sub object to another sub object, and so on within the same host asset.

- Group Index:- Group index is used to link any exact same assets to have a shared spawning behaviour.

title: Assets: Common Asset Principles
text:
when you have parented a sub object to another sub object, and so on within the same host asset.

- Group Index:- Group index is used to link any exact same assets to have a shared spawning behaviour.

- Grouped sub objects usually share the same probability parameters. When the game spawns these assets with the same group index, it will apply the same probability result to all of them. For example: assign 10 trees of the same type of tree with group index 0, and they will all spawn together or not, based on their grouped probability.
 - In a group with different probabilities, the lowest spawn probability is applied to the entire group.
 - Applying a Group Index of -1 to sub objects will cause them to act independently in terms of spawn rate.
 - Sub objects that are different, but are part of the same style set (like duct pieces) can have the same Group Index applied and spawn together as a group.

- Probability:- Dictates the possibility of the sub object appearing on the host asset per placement or spawn. Some of the base game props have probability set less than 100% to provide visual randomisation by default.
- Markers, and other important props should be set at 100 % probability at all times.

# Areas and Surfaces

## Component

title: Assets: Common Asset Principles
text:
ave probability set less than 100% to provide visual randomisation by default.
- Markers, and other important props should be set at 100 % probability at all times.

# Areas and Surfaces

## Component

Areas and surfaces can be found in their respective categories located in the asset browser. While there are decorative surfaces such as tiles or grass surfaces, Cities: Skylines II also features areas that are used to route pedestrians inside the asset lot. There are also special surface types that are used to clip the terrain or clear areas. Any added area or surface is listed insideObject Sub Areascomponent.

### Areas

Assets CommonPrinciplesAreas

Areas are functional spaces used by pedestrians to navigate inside the host asset. Areas, compared to paths and lanes, are open spaces. In other words, pedestrian pathfinding aims to disperse the pedestrian flow across the area, while still respecting the optimal pedestrian route. The transition between different areas are achieved by placing Pedestrian Access Location markers on the boundary of 2 separate areas..

The supported pedestrian areas are::

title: Assets: Common Asset Principles
text:
optimal pedestrian route. The transition between different areas are achieved by placing Pedestrian Access Location markers on the boundary of 2 separate areas..

The supported pedestrian areas are::

- Walking Area: - This is the most common area type for pedestrians. Walking areas are used to create pedestrian navigation and flow inside the host asset, usually between the sidewalk or path the host asset is located near, and end points within the asset, like Pedestrian Spawn Locations, or Bench props and other sub objects with activity locations.
- Hangaround Area: - This is the area where pedestrians can idle or create idle groups. Hangaround areas are also used on public transport assets to create functional waiting platforms for transit vehicles. Hangaround areas can still be used for simple navigation.
- Gympark Area: - Gympark area is the location where pedestrians can perform physical activities. Gympark areas can still be used for simple navigation.
- Park Area: - Park area is the location where pedestrians idle by lying on the floor, effectively ‘sunbathing’, practicing yoga and other simple park activities. Park areas can still be used for simple navigation.

### Using Areas

Assets CommonPrinciplesPropCollision

title: Assets: Common Asset Principles
text:
ing on the floor, effectively ‘sunbathing’, practicing yoga and other simple park activities. Park areas can still be used for simple navigation.

### Using Areas

Assets CommonPrinciplesPropCollision

- Once you’ve selected an area, make sure the “Binds overlapping items to a building” snapping option is toggled on.
- Areas are node based shapes, where you plot out multiple nodes to create a perimeter that connects back to the original node, creating an area for navigation.
- Since the game will aim to distribute pedestrians across the area, you should aim to keep the area clean of any props or objects. Pedestrians can walk anywhere inside the area, so if there are props overlapping the area, pedestrians may navigate through them.
- You can have disconnected and different areas across the host asset, for example a rooftop garden. The connection between these areas can be achieved with either Restricted or Pedestrian Spawn Locations acting as ‘doors’ between the areas.
- If you want to place dynamic props on places like balconies, you will need a walking area on the balcony for the dynamic prop to connect to and the area itself should be connected with a Restricted or Pedestrian Spawn Location that connects to another providing a path to the front of the asset. The dynamic prop should be positioned as close to the desired area as possible, as it will attempt to connect to the closest area - if there are multiple areas around the prop, make sure it connects to the correct area using the Pathfinding Debug System gizmo.
- For larger spaces such as backyards, gardens and so on, you can use either walking, hangaround, park or gympark areas.

title: Assets: Common Asset Principles
text:
sure it connects to the correct area using the Pathfinding Debug System gizmo.
- For larger spaces such as backyards, gardens and so on, you can use either walking, hangaround, park or gympark areas.

### Surfaces

Assets CommonPrinciplesSurfaces

Surfaces are mostly decorative areas that can be used on the building lot. The base game provides a selection of surfaces and variations for you to use on your buildings.

### Using Surfaces

- Once you’ve selected a surface, make sure the “Binds overlapping items to a building” snapping option is toggled on.
- The creation of a surface is identical to areas. Surfaces are node based shapes, where you plot out multiple nodes to create a perimeter that connects back to the original node, creating an area to fill with the decorative surface material.
- Unlike areas, surfaces can only be used on the terrain and they cannot be elevated.
- Placeholder surfaces can be used to randomize surface visuals from a predetermined pool of surface assets, similar to placeholder props.
- Surfaces often have a predetermined render order that may affect your usage.
- Surfaces cannot be removed by Clear Area.

### Special Use Cases

#### Clear Area

title: Assets: Common Asset Principles
text:
assets, similar to placeholder props.
- Surfaces often have a predetermined render order that may affect your usage.
- Surfaces cannot be removed by Clear Area.

### Special Use Cases

#### Clear Area

Clear Area is a unique area type that is used on building extensions in order to clean up the location in the host building lot on which the extension is being placed. It clears any prop, effect, decoration, network, lane or other areas, including within a limited height collision.

- Clear Area cannot delete surfaces.
- When you’re working with extension upgrades, you can add the host asset’s mesh as an additional, temporary mesh on the upgrade, then create the outline of the clear area where desired. You can then delete the main mesh from the extension.

#### Clip Surface

Clip Surface is used to create a precise void cut into the terrain. It can be used for assets featuring underground passages, or sunken elements such as parking lots, terraces or underground entrances. Clip Surface works like any other surface.

## Object Sub Area Parameters

Assets CommonPrinciplesSubAreaComponent

When you add an area or surface to an asset, the game automatically generates anObject Sub Areascomponent for it. The component is made similarly to the object container, and can be toggled at any time.

title: Assets: Common Asset Principles
text:
en you add an area or surface to an asset, the game automatically generates anObject Sub Areascomponent for it. The component is made similarly to the object container, and can be toggled at any time.

- Area Prefab:- Each area is listed as an element. You can check individual elements and swap your existing surfaces via the Area Prefab dropdown list.
- Node Positions:- An area comprises multiple nodes. Each node is defined by its 3D world space coordinates, relative to the host asset’s pivot point. These nodes are exposed to the creator inside the container called Node Positions.
- Parent Meshes:- For nodes and thus areas parented to a mesh, each node receives an identical element ID located in this list. The hierarchical value of nodes relative to meshes are identical as parent mesh index of props;

- Element value -1: is always the world terrain, any node assigned this will attempt to match the height of the terrain the host asset is placed or spawned on.
 - Element value 0: the node is parented to the mesh listed as ‘element 0’ in the meshes list of the host asset.
 - Element value 1: the node is parented to the mesh listed as ‘element 1’ in the meshes list of the host asset, and so on.

Note: For transit stations’ hangaround areas, make sure all area nodes on the platform locations are at identical heights and parented to the same mesh.

# Networks and Paths

title: Assets: Common Asset Principles
text:
e host asset, and so on.

Note: For transit stations’ hangaround areas, make sure all area nodes on the platform locations are at identical heights and parented to the same mesh.

# Networks and Paths

Assets CommonPrinciplesAreasvsNets

## Component

Networks and paths are functional lot elements that ensure proper pedestrian and vehicle routing around a host asset. Unlike areas, networks are about precision. They provide lane bound pathfinding, which makes them a good alternative for routing pedestrians through locations that require precise pathfinding, such as overpasses and corridors. Networks will usually attach to networks outside of the host asset, without the need of an access location marker.

Routing vehicles is only possible via networks and the lanes within them. There are also combined networks that allow integrated routing of both vehicles and pedestrians. These are useful for areas like garages where vehicle accessibility is needed and pedestrians are expected to leave the garage via road access. Any network or path that is added to the building is listed underObject Sub Netscomponent.

### Common Networks

Assets CommonPrinciplesInivisiblePaths

These are common networks that are often used on assets:

title: Assets: Common Asset Principles
text:
rk or path that is added to the building is listed underObject Sub Netscomponent.

### Common Networks

Assets CommonPrinciplesInivisiblePaths

These are common networks that are often used on assets:

- Invisible Car Path - 1xOneway:- Contains a single, one-way car lane, no pedestrian lanes. Good for narrow driving routes that loop around the asset. Invisible when the host asset appears in-game.
- Invisible Car Path - 1xOneway 1xBay:- Similar to Invisible Car Path - 1xOneway but with an extra parallel parking lane adjacent to the main car lane. No pedestrian lanes. Good for narrow driving routes that also require some form of parking, or temporary parking like taxi stands. Invisible when the host asset appears in-game.
- Invisible Car Path - 1xTwoway:- Contains a single, two-way car lane, no pedestrian lanes. Good for a driveway, as it allows a car to enter and exit a garage. Invisible when the host asset appears in-game.
- Invisible Car Path - 2xTwoway:- Contains two separate car lanes, one driving forward, one driving backward and no pedestrian lanes. Good for street-like vehicle access that may have more traffic than a single lane could handle. Invisible when the host asset appears in-game.
- Invisible Road Path - 1xOneway:- Contains a single, one-way car lane with two-way pedestrian lanes on either side of the car lane. Good for narrow driving routes that loop around the asset. Invisible when the host asset appears in-game.
- Invisible Road Path - 1xTwoway:- Contains a single, two-way car lane with two-way pedestrian lanes on either side of the car lane. Good for a driveway, as it allows a car to enter and exit a garage. Invisible when the host asset appears in-game.
- Invisible Road Path - 2xTwoway:- Contains two separate car lanes, one driving forward, one driving backward and with two-way pedestrian lanes on either side of the pair of car lanes. Good for street-like vehicle access that may have more traffic than a single lane could handle. Invisible when the host asset appears in-game.
- Invisible Road Path - 2xTwoway 2xPerpendicular:- Contains two separate car lanes, one driving forward, one driving backward and with two-way pedestrian lanes on either side of the pair of car lanes. Also has on either side, perpendicular, procedurally generated parking decals that allow vehicles to park. Good for custom street-like parking lot spaces. The car and pedestrian lanes are Invisible when the host asset appears in-game, the parking decals stay visible.

title: Assets: Common Asset Principles
text:
ing decals that allow vehicles to park. Good for custom street-like parking lot spaces. The car and pedestrian lanes are Invisible when the host asset appears in-game, the parking decals stay visible.

### Using Networks

Assets CommonPrinciplesParking

- Once you’ve selected a network to use, make sure the “Binds overlapping items to a building” snapping option is toggled on.
- Networks do not require any other marker to be connected with the outside lot, they work based on proximity: it is sufficient to have one node close to the edge of the lot to allow the network to be accessible.
- Networks featuring both pedestrian and vehicle routing can intersect with networks which provide either pedestrian or vehicle only routing.
- Networks containing pedestrian lanes can be connected to pedestrian areas, provided there is an Pedestrian Access Location marker connecting the network and the area.
- Certain props will require proximity to networks, usually parking lot markers, or vehicle spawn markers. These markers need to be close to the network as much as possible to ensure vehicle routing to the marker is accurate.
Note: When using the Pathfinding Debug System gizmo, car lanes inside of networks are shown as white lines and arrows, pedestrian lanes are shown as green lines.

## Sub Nets Parameters

Assets CommonPrinciplesSubNetsComponent

title: Assets: Common Asset Principles
text:
inding Debug System gizmo, car lanes inside of networks are shown as white lines and arrows, pedestrian lanes are shown as green lines.

## Sub Nets Parameters

Assets CommonPrinciplesSubNetsComponent

Object Sub Netcomponent contains all networks placed in the building lot. Each network segment is separated into elements and listed as shown in the image. The asset creator has the ability to edit dragged segments and adjust them manually.

- Invert When:- This is a dropdown above the listed elements of Sub Nets, and is applied to all networks on this component. Select from the options to determine when the direction of the lanes within a network should be inverted (flipped). Usually assets made in the editor follow the RHD lane direction, so if you wish to support LHD you would select “LeftHandTraffic” in the dropdown so when a player loads the asset in a LHD save, the lanes will correctly invert and match the LHD setting.
- Net Prefab:- Each network is listed as an element. You can check individual elements and swap your existing networks via the Net Prefab dropdown list. Swapping segments like this may break the whole composition of the network, so use with caution.
- Bezier Curve:- All networks, lanes and fences in Cities: Skylines II follow theBezier Curvemethod.

title: Assets: Common Asset Principles
text:
t. Swapping segments like this may break the whole composition of the network, so use with caution.
- Bezier Curve:- All networks, lanes and fences in Cities: Skylines II follow theBezier Curvemethod.

- Point A and Drepresent the end nodes of your segment.
 - Point B and Care mid-control points, these help to define any curve to a segment.
 - Point B and Ccannot overlap with Point A and D or share identical coordinates.

- Node Indexdemonstrates the connection state between nodes and their segments. If nodes share the same index number between different segments, then it means those nodes are intersections between the segments in question. Node Index is generated based on the order segments are drawn. Indexes can be re-generated if segments are deleted in the asset, so the game will re-index remaining nodes.
- Parent Meshindicates which mesh element it is attached to.

- -1:- is always the world terrain, any node assigned this will attempt to match the height of the terrain the host asset is placed or spawned on.
 - 0:- the node is parented to the mesh listed as ‘element 0’ in the meshes list of the host asset.
 - 1:- the node is parented to the mesh listed as ‘element 1’ in the meshes list of the host asset, and so on.

- Upgrades:- are used to assign and trigger different states on the segment such as its elevation.

# Lanes & Fences

## Component

Lanes are decorative segments that behave similarly to networks. Lanes are considered more flexible and easier to use - for example: in place of manually repeating a prop along a length - as it solves the problem of aligning props individually to create a wall. Lanes also behave like networks, offering some degree of flexibility to expand or shrink the mesh, instead of clipping wall props into each other to make up a shorter distance.

When you add a lane to your host asset it will automatically create aObject Sub Lanescomponent.

### Common Types of Lanes

These are common types of lanes that can be used in assets:

- Fences
- Hedges
- Road Markings
- Cables
Note: There are other assets that are mechanically lanes, but should only be used as a part of a network asset, not standalone. If used standalone, they can cause extreme pathfinding issues for the host asset.

### Using Lanes for Decoration

- Unlike props, lanes have a collision box and allow adjacent fences on bordering lots to delete each other if there’s an overlap. This removes the z-fighting issue on lot walls since only one fence will be kept, shared by two adjacent lots.

title: Assets: Common Asset Principles
text:
x and allow adjacent fences on bordering lots to delete each other if there’s an overlap. This removes the z-fighting issue on lot walls since only one fence will be kept, shared by two adjacent lots.

- The collision box depends on the mesh, but it is usually around 2m width from the centerline.

- Some decorative lanes have placeholder variants. These behave similar to placeholder props, offering some visual variety by randomizing per spawn/placement of the host asset.
- Lanes can be dragged on elevated surfaces such as balconies or rooftops depending on your snapping options.

## Sub Lanes Parameters

Object Sub Lanescomponent contains all lanes placed in the building lot. Each lane segment is separated into elements just like the Object Sub Nets component. The asset creator has the ability to edit dragged segments and adjust them manually.

- Lane Prefab:- Each lane is listed as an element. You can check individual elements and swap your existing lanes via the Lane Prefab dropdown list.
- Bezier Curve:- All networks, lanes and fences in Cities: Skylines II follow theBezier Curvemethod.

- Point A and Drepresent the end nodes of your lane segment.
 - Point B and Care mid-control points, these help to define any curve to a lane segment.
 - Point B and Ccannot overlap with Point A and D or share identical coordinates.

title: Assets: Common Asset Principles
text:
nodes of your lane segment.
 - Point B and Care mid-control points, these help to define any curve to a lane segment.
 - Point B and Ccannot overlap with Point A and D or share identical coordinates.

- Node Indexdemonstrates the connection state between nodes and their segments. If nodes share the same index number between different segments, then it means those nodes are intersections between the segments in question. Node Index is generated based on the order segments are drawn. Indexes can be re-generated if segments are deleted in the asset, so the game will re-index remaining nodes.
- Parent Meshindicates which mesh element the lane is attached to.

- -1:- is always the world terrain, any lane node assigned this will attempt to match the height of the terrain the host asset is placed or spawned on.
 - 0:- the lane node is parented to the mesh listed as ‘element 0’ in the meshes list of the host asset.
 - 1:- the lane node is parented to the mesh listed as ‘element 1’ in the meshes list of the host asset, and so on.

# Effects

Assets CommonPrinciplesEffects

## Component

Cities: Skylines II buildings can be decorated with various effects to give a visual or audio feedback to the player when the asset is operational or impacted by an incident.
Effects that can be assigned to a host asset can be found inside the Effects category. Any propped effect is listed under theEffect Sourcecomponent on the host asset.

### Common Effects

- Fire and Smoke Effects:- These effects are animated sprites that are placed on the asset to visualise a fire outbreak.
- Sounds:- Sound effects are often used for service and signature buildings. Sound effects provide sound on 3 axes based on distance from the effect marker. As such, they should be placed relatively at the center of the asset mesh or where the sound makes the most sense.
- Light Sources:- Light sources can be placed independently on an asset to emit light.

### Using Different Effects

title: Assets: Common Asset Principles
text:
relatively at the center of the asset mesh or where the sound makes the most sense.
- Light Sources:- Light sources can be placed independently on an asset to emit light.

### Using Different Effects

- Smoke and ember effects can be used to simulate fire.
- When adding fire effects to an asset, try to disperse them across the asset realistically. If there are multiple windows sharing the same parallax interior (as in, it’s the same room) then they should all have matching fire or smoke effects to reflect they are the same room.
- Generator props, AC units, doors are good locations to add fire or smoke effects.
- Very tall buildings can have their sound effects higher off the ground.
- Sound effects are not needed on individual spawnable buildings, as their zone prefab assigns their sound effects.
- Sound effects are usually named after the base game asset they are used on, so they can be searched via prefab names, with the effect category filtering (“PoliceStation”, “SignatureOffice” etc.).
- You should only place one sound per asset.
- Light Source effects should be used carefully and not excessively. Too many light sources in a small space will cause rendering artifacts.

## Effect Parameters

Fire, sound and light effects share the same set of options. They can be customized by individually selecting the effect on the host asset or using the list in the Effects Component.

title: Assets: Common Asset Principles
text:
ffect Parameters

Fire, sound and light effects share the same set of options. They can be customized by individually selecting the effect on the host asset or using the list in the Effects Component.

- Effect:- container lists the name of the effect prefab and can be swapped with any other effect from the dropdown list.
- Position Offset:- indicates the location of the effect gizmo with respect to the host asset.
- Rotation:- is used to rotate the effect, it can be used to fine tune light effects.
- Scale:- is not used for effects at the moment.
- Intensity:- is used to adjust the emission of light effects. Intensity can be set per light effect and doesn't carry over other instances of the same prefab effect.
- Parent Mesh:- indicates which mesh element the effect is attached to.
- Animation Index- is used for effects working with animations. Usually this is left as default.

# Thumbnails

## Component

When an asset is designed to show in the in-game menu, a thumbnail needs to be assigned to the asset. In Cities: Skylines II, asset thumbnails are all 1:1 ratio, usually 128x128 pixels. Adding theUI Objectcomponent to an asset allows you to choose an appropriate thumbnail.

## UI Object

title: Assets: Common Asset Principles
text:
to the asset. In Cities: Skylines II, asset thumbnails are all 1:1 ratio, usually 128x128 pixels. Adding theUI Objectcomponent to an asset allows you to choose an appropriate thumbnail.

## UI Object

- Group:- this is a dropdown that allows you to select your desired UI category the asset should be assigned to in the in-game menu. It’s a good practice to compare UI Objects to built-in assets.

- For upgrades, the group must always be set to ‘none’, which is the default group when adding a new UI Object.

- Priority:- this is a number that assigns the order the asset will appear in compared to other assets in the same menu. Usually priority is determined by the creator, arranging it amongst existing built-in assets based on relative size, cost, maintenance and how powerful it is within its own category of assets. If an asset has the same priority as another, the game will show them grouped together in the menu in a random order.

- For upgrades, the priority can be set to determine the order in the upgrades menu on the host asset.

- Icon:- the creator doesn’t usually need to be concerned with the url, unless you wish to re-use an existing icon from a built-in asset. In which case you’d locate the url of that icon and paste it here. Otherwise you would use the icon selector by clicking the default icon:
- Icon selector:

title: Assets: Common Asset Principles
text:
-use an existing icon from a built-in asset. In which case you’d locate the url of that icon and paste it here. Otherwise you would use the icon selector by clicking the default icon:
- Icon selector:

- Opens a list view of all .png images already located in the`..AppData\LocalLow\Colossal Order\Cities Skylines II`directory.
 - “select from disk” allows you to find and select any other supported file type - including .svg - locatedoutsideof the`..AppData\LocalLow\Colossal Order\Cities Skylines II`directory.

- Is Debug Object:- should remain unchecked.
