title: Assets: Import and Setup Aging Trees
text:
# Assets: Import and Setup Aging Trees

Source: https://cs2.paradoxwikis.com/Assets:_Import_and_Setup_Aging_Trees

Paradox logo black square Verified by Paradox Interactive
This article has been verified by Paradox Interactive for version 1.5.6 f1 of the game.

## Importing and Editor Setup

This page walks through importing your tree into the game and configuring every component in the in-game editor. Before continuing, ensure your FBX files, textures, WindFlutterMap, and settings.json files are correctly prepared as described in Asset Pipeline: Aging Trees.

### Importing

Open the in-game editor and click the Asset Import icon in the toolbar. Navigate to your asset folder. For the Prefab Preset, select Aging Tree and click import.

The importer will process each age stage subfolder it finds inside your asset folder. When the import is complete you will have the option to place your Tree asset, which automatically has Render Prefabs assigned to it for each age stage.

### Prefab Structure

Every imported aging tree consists of a Static Object Prefab and 6 Render Prefabs. Understanding this structure makes the editor setup easier to follow.

title: Assets: Import and Setup Aging Trees
text:
o it for each age stage.

### Prefab Structure

Every imported aging tree consists of a Static Object Prefab and 6 Render Prefabs. Understanding this structure makes the editor setup easier to follow.

- Static Object Prefab (e.g. OakTree01) — The prefab players interact with. Handles all gameplay and placement logic, and contains a mesh list with one entry per age stage.
- Render Prefab (e.g. OakTreeAdult01 Mesh) — The visual definition for one age stage. Controls LOD distances, color variation, and seasonal color changes. One is automatically created per age stage on import.

You configure each type separately in the editor inspector.

### Finding Your Render Prefabs

There are two ways to find your tree's Render Prefabs in the editor:

Place your tree asset in the editor worldspace.

1. Find it in the Object section of the workspace panel.
2. Expand the asset's dropdown arrow to reveal its assigned Render Prefabs.
3. Select the Render Prefab you wish to configure to inspect it.

Or use the Asset Browser:

1. Open the Asset Browser and select Render Prefabs from the left panel.
2. Search for the name of your mesh — the Render Prefab will be named after it if you followed the import naming conventions.
3. Select it to load it into the workspace panel for inspection.

## Tree Mesh Render Prefab

title: Assets: Import and Setup Aging Trees
text:
name of your mesh — the Render Prefab will be named after it if you followed the import naming conventions.
3. Select it to load it into the workspace panel for inspection.

## Tree Mesh Render Prefab

Open an age stage Render Prefab (e.g., OakTreeAdult01 Mesh) in the inspector. This prefab has three components: LodProperties, ColorProperties, and SeasonColorFilter.

### LOD Properties

Controls how far away the tree can be seen and where it transitions to its lower-detail versions.

Field Description

Bias A float multiplier that adjusts the LOD transition distance. Negative values make the full-detail mesh visible at greater distances (it stays detailed longer). Positive values cause it to switch to the lower LOD sooner. For most trees, leave this at 0.

Shadow Bias The same bias adjustment, but applied only to the shadow rendering LOD. Leave at 0 unless you have a specific reason to change it.

LOD Meshes A list of Render Prefab references pointing to the lower-detail versions of this mesh. The import pipeline fills this in automatically — it will contain one entry: the auto-generated impostor (LOD1). Do not remove this entry. Note: The Render Prefab at this level represents LOD0 (the highest-detail mesh). The LOD Meshes list contains one entry — the auto-generated impostor, which serves as LOD1.

### Color Properties

title: Assets: Import and Setup Aging Trees
text:
ntry. Note: The Render Prefab at this level represents LOD0 (the highest-detail mesh). The LOD Meshes list contains one entry — the auto-generated impostor, which serves as LOD1.

### Color Properties

Defines the color variations that can appear on this tree. In-game, the game randomly selects from these variations and applies them via the ControlMask channels, giving individual trees slightly different coloring even within the same species.

This component is set up per-age-stage. For a full aging tree, you will configure ColorProperties on each age stage's Render Prefab.

#### Color Variations

Each entry is a set of three colors, one for each colormask channel. These are the colors that will be multiplied over the BaseColor in the areas painted white in the ControlMask R, G, and B channels respectively.

Field Description

Colors [0] Color applied to areas white in ControlMask Red channel (Colormask 1).

Colors [1] Color applied to areas white in ControlMask Green channel (Colormask 2).

Colors [2] Color applied to areas white in ControlMask Blue channel (Colormask 3).

title: Assets: Import and Setup Aging Trees
text:
Mask Red channel (Colormask 1).

Colors [1] Color applied to areas white in ControlMask Green channel (Colormask 2).

Colors [2] Color applied to areas white in ControlMask Blue channel (Colormask 3).

Variation Group The name of the group this color set belongs to. Assign color sets to groups named Spring, Summer, Autumn, and Winter using the dropdown list so the SeasonColorFilter can activate the right ones for each season. The list of groups in this dropdown is populated from the Variation Groups elements detailed below.

For an oak tree, you would typically add four sets of color variations — one for each season — assigned to their respective groups. The values from the OakTree01 example:

- Spring / Summer: muted greens
- Autumn: warm oranges and yellows
- Winter: desaturated pale tones
Tip: You may be able to add multiple Color Sets to the same season group for more discrete color variety, on top of the HSV randomization ranges.

#### Channels Binding

Maps the three logical color channels (0, 1, 2) to which color index they read from the variation set. For trees this is almost always a direct 0→R, 1→G, 2→B mapping to the ControlMask channels. The Can Be Modified By External checkbox controls whether an external system (such as a brand or parent prefab) is allowed to override that channel's color. For trees, leave this unchecked on all three channels.

title: Assets: Import and Setup Aging Trees
text:
Modified By External checkbox controls whether an external system (such as a brand or parent prefab) is allowed to override that channel's color. For trees, leave this unchecked on all three channels.

#### Variation Groups

A Variation Group is a named container that groups one or more Color Sets together and controls how the game selects and synchronizes them. For an aging tree, you will typically have four Variation Group elements in this list — one for each season: Spring, Summer, Autumn, Winter. Each new group is added to the dropdown on the color set elements based on the string in the "Name" field mentioned below.

Each Variation Group element has the following settings:

Field Description

Name A plain text string that identifies this group. The SeasonColorFilter and the Color Variation entries both reference groups by this name — the game matches them by string comparison at initialization time, not by prefab reference.

This is why Name is a text field rather than an asset picker: groups are registered dynamically at runtime by the color system, allowing multiple Render Prefabs (such as the six age stages of a tree) to all reference the same group by name without needing direct asset links between them.

Use the names Spring, Summer, Autumn, and Winter so they match what the SeasonColorFilter expects.

title: Assets: Import and Setup Aging Trees
text:
of a tree) to all reference the same group by name without needing direct asset links between them.

Use the names Spring, Summer, Autumn, and Winter so they match what the SeasonColorFilter expects.

Probability How likely this group is to be randomly selected when no external override is active. For seasonal tree groups, set this to 0.

A value of 0 means the group will never be chosen by random chance on its own — it can only be activated by the SeasonColorFilter, which overrides this probability to 100 when the matching season is active.

Setting it to 0 ensures seasonal colors never appear outside their correct season due to random selection.

Mesh Sync Mode Controls how the color assigned to this group is synchronized across multiple mesh elements on the same object — for example, keeping the trunk and foliage of a tree in the same color variation. This is a flags field, meaning multiple values can be combined. The available flags are:

title: Assets: Import and Setup Aging Trees
text:
elements on the same object — for example, keeping the trunk and foliage of a tree in the same color variation. This is a flags field, meaning multiple values can be combined. The available flags are:

- SameGroup — This mesh element will use the same named Variation Group (e.g. Spring) as a previously processed mesh element on the same object. Ensures all parts of the tree draw from the same active seasonal Variation Group.
- SameIndex — This mesh element will use the same color index (slot) within the group as a previously processed mesh element. Ensures all parts use the exact same Color Set, not just the same group.
- DifferentGroup — This mesh element will only apply if a different group was used on a previous mesh element. Used for parts that should not share a group with others.
- DifferentIndex — This mesh element will only apply if a different color index was used on a previous mesh element. Used to force color diversity across parts.
- SyncRangeVariation — Uses the same random seed for HSV variation as a previously processed mesh element, so hue/saturation/brightness shifts are identical across parts rather than independently randomized.

title: Assets: Import and Setup Aging Trees
text:
angeVariation — Uses the same random seed for HSV variation as a previously processed mesh element, so hue/saturation/brightness shifts are identical across parts rather than independently randomized.

For aging trees, the base game uses SameGroup + SameIndex combined. A tree has two material slots — trunk (VegRoot) and foliage (VegLeaves) — which the color system treats as separate surface elements. SameGroup ensures both slots draw from the same active seasonal group. SameIndex ensures they use the exact same Color Set within that group. Without both flags, the trunk and foliage could receive different color variations independently, producing inconsistent results.

Override Randomness When enabled, this group uses its own Variation Ranges and Alpha Ranges values instead of the component-level global values. Leave unchecked for standard trees — use the component-level ranges instead.

#### Variation Ranges (on Color Properties component-level)

These are the global randomization ranges applied to the Hue, Saturation, and Brightness of every color set, across all groups, unless a specific group has Override Randomness enabled. Each value is on a 0–100 scale and applied multiplicatively — a value of N means each tree instance's color is multiplied by a random factor between (1 − N/100) and (1 + N/100). The result is a different random multiplier per tree, applied at spawn.

title: Assets: Import and Setup Aging Trees
text:
licatively — a value of N means each tree instance's color is multiplied by a random factor between (1 − N/100) and (1 + N/100). The result is a different random multiplier per tree, applied at spawn.

- Hue Randomness — Hue is the dominant color tone on the color wheel (the difference between green, yellow-green, orange, and so on). A non-zero value randomly shifts the hue of each instance's colormask tint by up to ±x%, wrapping around the color wheel so no hue values are lost. For trees, a small value (1–3) creates subtle tonal differences between individual trees — some slightly warmer, some slightly cooler — without noticeably departing from the intended season color. The base game oak uses 0, keeping hue consistent.
- Saturation Randomness — Saturation controls how vivid or muted the color appears. A non-zero value randomly scales the saturation of each instance's tint. The base game oak uses 0, keeping saturation consistent.
- Brightness Randomness — Brightness (Value in HSV) controls how light or dark the color appears. A non-zero value randomly scales the brightness of each instance's tint. The base game oak uses 2, giving each tree a small brightness shift that makes forests look naturally uneven rather than uniformly lit. Start with small values (0–5) and check the result in-game.
Alpha RangesThese control random variation in the opa city of the tint applied through each of the three ControlMask channels. Each input is on the same 0–100 scale as the Variation Ranges above.

title: Assets: Import and Setup Aging Trees
text:
ame.
Alpha RangesThese control random variation in the opa city of the tint applied through each of the three ControlMask channels. Each input is on the same 0–100 scale as the Variation Ranges above.

- Alpha 0 Randomness — Varies the opa city of the tint applied through ControlMask R (Colormask 1).
- Alpha 1 Randomness — Varies the opa city of the tint applied through ControlMask G (Colormask 2).
- Alpha 2 Randomness — Varies the opa city of the tint applied through ControlMask B (Colormask 3).
For standard trees, leave all three at 0. The colormask tints are designed to apply at full opa city — varying them would make some trees' foliage tinting appear stronger or weaker than intended, which is difficult to control predictably. Alpha randomization is more relevant for assets where partial or variable tint strength is a deliberate design goal.

External Color Source

Leave at the default (Brand) for trees. This field is only relevant for assets such as commercial buildings where an external prefab can inject brand colors.

### Season Color Filter

Connects the color variation groups defined in ColorProperties to the game's season system, so the tree changes color across Spring, Summer, Autumn, and Winter.

Field Description

Season Blend Mode How the game transitions between season color states:

title: Assets: Import and Setup Aging Trees
text:
Properties to the game's season system, so the tree changes color across Spring, Summer, Autumn, and Winter.

Field Description

Season Blend Mode How the game transitions between season color states:

- None (−1) — No seasonal blending. The color switches abruptly when the season changes.
- Colors — The color values themselves are interpolated (blended) between the outgoing and incoming season colors during transition.
- Probability — The probability of selecting each variation group is blended during transition, so trees gradually shift between the two seasonal appearances.

Color Filters A list of filter entries, one per season. Each entry maps a season to one or more variation groups.

Each entry in the Color Filters list:

Field Description

Season Filter The SeasonPrefab this entry applies to. Pick from the available seasons (Spring, Summer, Autumn, Winter).

Variation Groups One or more group names from ColorProperties that should be active during this season. For a standard setup, add one entry per season pointing to the matching group name (e.g., Season Filter = Spring → Variation Groups = ["Spring"]).

Override Probability The probability value forced onto the variation group during this season. Set to 100 to guarantee this group is always selected when its season is active. Set to −1 to use the group's default probability instead.

title: Assets: Import and Setup Aging Trees
text:
ity value forced onto the variation group during this season. Set to 100 to guarantee this group is always selected when its season is active. Set to −1 to use the group's default probability instead.

For a standard four-season tree, you will have four entries: Spring → ["Spring"] probability 100, Summer → ["Summer"] probability 100, Autumn → ["Autumn"] probability 100, Winter → ["Winter"] probability 100.

Differences by age stage: The Dead and Stump stages should not change color with seasons in the same way as living stages, since they have no leaves. For Dead and Stump, you can either:

- Configure a single neutral variation group (e.g., a desaturated gray-brown bark color) and assign it to all four seasons, or
- Configure separate Winter and non-Winter entries to account for any snow-related appearance change.

The Stump stage's Render Prefab may not need ColorProperties or SeasonColorFilter at all if the stump only shows trunk geometry without color masking — leaving them empty is acceptable.

## Static Object Prefab

Open the Static Object Prefab (e.g., OakTree01) in the inspector. This is the top-level prefab that players select and place from the UI. It contains all the game logic and all six age stage mesh references.

### Name and Localisation

title: Assets: Import and Setup Aging Trees
text:
., OakTree01) in the inspector. This is the top-level prefab that players select and place from the UI. It contains all the game logic and all six age stage mesh references.

### Name and Localisation

- Name — The internal identifier for your prefab. This must be unique.
- Localisation — Display name and description entries for each supported language. The localised name and description is shown to players in the UI if the tree is given a UI component.

### Mesh List

The mesh list contains one entry per age stage, six total. Each entry references a Render Prefab and uses a Require State value to indicate which growth stage activates it.

Render Prefab Require State

OakTreeChild01 Mesh Child

OakTreeTeen01 Mesh Teen

OakTreeAdult01 Mesh Adult

OakTreeElderly01 Mesh Elderly

OakTreeDead01 Mesh Dead

OakTreeStump01 Mesh Stump

These are set automatically by the importer. If you need to reassign or reorder them, match the RequireState value to the table above.

### Circular (checkbox)

Tells the placement system that the footprint of this object is circular rather than rectangular. When checked, the tree occupies a round footprint for collision and placement purposes, which is correct for trees since their canopy and root area are radially symmetric. Leave checked.

### Tree Object

Marks this prefab as a tree and defines its resource value.

Field Description

title: Assets: Import and Setup Aging Trees
text:
ses, which is correct for trees since their canopy and root area are radially symmetric. Leave checked.

### Tree Object

Marks this prefab as a tree and defines its resource value.

Field Description

Wood Amount The amount of wood resource this tree provides when harvested by a forestry industry. Default is 3000. Set higher for large old-growth species, lower for small or decorative trees. If your tree is purely decorative and should not contribute to forestry, you may lower this to 0 or leave it at the default. Note: Setting a wood amount is recommended as the game allows players to set a tree as decoration, removing the wood amount per instance placed as decoration.

### Placeable Object

Field Description

Construction Cost The in-game cost to place this tree from the UI. Used when the player manually places a tree.

XP Reward XP awarded on placement. Trees are usually set to 0.

### Service Object

Associates this tree with a city service. For trees, this should reference the Parks and Recreation service (or equivalent), which is what causes the tree to contribute to park service coverage in the city. Use the same ServicePrefab as existing base game trees.

### UI Object

Configures how this tree appears in the player's toolbar and asset browser.

Field Description

Group The UI category panel this tree will appear in. (e.g., the Trees category in the landscaping menu).

title: Assets: Import and Setup Aging Trees
text:
onfigures how this tree appears in the player's toolbar and asset browser.

Field Description

Group The UI category panel this tree will appear in. (e.g., the Trees category in the landscaping menu).

Priority Sort order within the group. Lower numbers appear earlier in the list.

Icon Path to the icon representing this tree in the UI. Displayed in the toolbar when the tree is selected.

Is Debug Object Leave unchecked. Debug objects are only shown in debug builds.

### Standing Object

Defines the collision volume of the tree. The X and Z values set the collision radius, which should be larger than the trunk itself. The Y value defines the height of the collision volume — this corresponds roughly to where the tree's canopy begins, representing the point at which the tree starts colliding with other objects above ground. It is typically kept relatively small so that trees can be placed closer together without their collision volumes overlapping excessively.

### Unlockable

Controls whether this tree is available to the player from the start or locked behind a progression milestone.

Field Description

Require All All listed prefabs must be unlocked.

Require Any Any one of the listed prefabs must be unlocked.

Ignore Dependencies Bypass auto-generated dependency checks.

For a custom tree available from the start, leave both lists empty.

### Sub Object Default Probability

title: Assets: Import and Setup Aging Trees
text:
listed prefabs must be unlocked.

Ignore Dependencies Bypass auto-generated dependency checks.

For a custom tree available from the start, leave both lists empty.

### Sub Object Default Probability

Field Description

Default Probability 0–100. How frequently the game spawns this tree species when placed as a sub object. The base game oak uses 70.

Rotation Symmetry Defines what rotations are valid when this tree is placed as a sub-object. Select from the dropdown:

- None — The tree is always placed at its default orientation. No rotation is applied.
- _180 — The tree can be placed at 0° or 180°. Two possible orientations.
- _90 — The tree can be placed at 0°, 90°, 180°, or 270°. Four possible orientations.
- _45 — The tree can be placed at 45° increments. Eight possible orientations.
- Any — The tree can be placed at any angle. Full continuous rotation. Use this for trees, since their canopy is radially symmetric. The base game oak uses Any.

### Effect Source

Attaches effects to the tree. The base game oak has two effects attached: a VFX fire effect and an audio SFX for fire looping.

Field Description

Effects A list of effect entries. Each entry defines one effect attached to this tree.

Each entry in the Effects list:

Field Description

Effect Reference to the EffectPrefab to play.

title: Assets: Import and Setup Aging Trees
text:
d Description

Effects A list of effect entries. Each entry defines one effect attached to this tree.

Each entry in the Effects list:

Field Description

Effect Reference to the EffectPrefab to play.

Position Offset The 3D coordinates of the effect relative to the parent prefab's origin.

Rotation Rotates the effect on the X, Y, and Z axes relative to the parent prefab's origin.

Scale Scale of the effect. 1, 1, 1 = no change.

Intensity Strength/multiplier of the effect. Must not be 0 (0 is automatically corrected to 1 at runtime with a warning).

Parent Mesh The mesh index this effect is parented to. 0 = the first mesh in the parent's mesh list.

Animation Index Animation Index is not used for Trees.

Return to: Asset Creation GuideSee also: Asset Pipeline: Aging Trees

title: Assets: Import and Setup Surfaces
text:
# Assets: Import and Setup Surfaces

Source: https://cs2.paradoxwikis.com/Assets:_Import_and_Setup_Surfaces

Paradox logo black square Verified by Paradox Interactive
This article has been verified by Paradox Interactive for version 1.5.6 f1 of the game.

## Prefab Setup

### Starting From an Existing Surface

The easiest way to create a surface is to duplicate an existing surface prefab. You can also create a new prefab from scratch and manually add the required components, but duplicating an existing surface is recommended as it ensures all components are present and correctly configured from the start.

Choose your starting prefab based on the intended visual character of your surface:

- Hard or urban surfaces (concrete, pavement, tiling) → duplicate Concrete Surface 01
- Natural or organic surfaces (grass, dirt, agriculture) → duplicate Grass Surface 01

Rename the duplicated prefab to your surface name before making any changes, and save the prefab.

### Configuring the Surface Prefab Component

The Surface Prefab component is the top-level component on your prefab and contains identity and overlay colour settings.

Name and Localisation

- Name — The internal identifier for your prefab. This must be unique.
- Localisation — Display name entries for each supported language. The localised name is shown to players in the UI if the surface is given a UI component.

title: Assets: Import and Setup Surfaces
text:
tifier for your prefab. This must be unique.
- Localisation — Display name entries for each supported language. The localised name is shown to players in the UI if the surface is given a UI component.

Overlay Colours These four colours control the flat polygon overlay drawn over the surface area in-game when the surface tool is active or when the surface is selected. They are separate from the textured rendering configured in the Rendered Area component.

Field Description

Color Fill colour of the surface polygon overlay in its normal, unselected state. Alpha controls the opa city of the overlay.

Edge Color Outline colour of the polygon boundary in its normal, unselected state.

Selection Color Fill colour of the polygon overlay when the surface is selected by the player.

Selected Edge Color Outline colour of the polygon boundary when the surface is selected. Known Issue: Currently the color picker type for these values is missing the alpha slider, so semi transparency is not selectable for the overlay colors. This bug has been reported to the developers.

### Importing Textures

Each texture slot uses a dedicated importer that automatically applies the correct shaders.

Select your duplicated prefab in the workspace and locate the Rendered Area component in the inspector panel. Import each texture into its corresponding slot:

Slot Import File

title: Assets: Import and Setup Surfaces
text:
s the correct shaders.

Select your duplicated prefab in the workspace and locate the Rendered Area component in the inspector panel. Import each texture into its corresponding slot:

Slot Import File

Base Color Map ExampleSurface_BaseColor.png

Normal Map ExampleSurface_Normal.png

Mask Map ExampleSurface_MaskMap.png

### Configuring the Rendered Area Component

The Rendered Area component controls the visual appearance and edge behaviour of the surface. Basic Parameters

- Roundness (0.0–1.0) — Controls how rounded the corners of the drawn polygon appear.

- 0.5 → Geometric, urban character
 - 1.0 → Fully organic, natural character

- Renderer Priority — Determines render order when two surfaces overlap. Less negative values render on top of more negative values.

- Default range used by game assets: -95 to -100
 - Set relative to what other surfaces your surface is expected to coexist or overlap with.

- Decal Layer Mask - set from a dropdown which types of surfaces your custom surface will render on, multiple layers can be enabled simultaneously.

Slot Import File

Terrain The surface renders on the terrain mesh. This is the correct setting for virtually all ground surfaces and is the default.

The surface renders over road surfaces. Enable this if you want your surface to paint over roads rather than being clipped by them.

title: Assets: Import and Setup Surfaces
text:
rrect setting for virtually all ground surfaces and is the default.

The surface renders over road surfaces. Enable this if you want your surface to paint over roads rather than being clipped by them.

Buildings The surface renders over building footprints.

Vehicles The surface renders over vehicles.

Creatures The surface renders over pedestrians and character models.

Other The surface renders over geometry that has been flagged as Other.

- For almost all custom surfaces, leaving this at the default Terrain is correct. Roads would only be relevant if you specifically want the surface to visually cover roads drawn inside its boundary — for example, an agricultural surface that should paint over a farm track.

Texture Tiling

- UV Scale — Controls how large the texture tiles across the drawn surface. Lower values produce larger, more expansive tiling. Higher values produce tighter, denser tiling.

- Natural surfaces (grass, agriculture): 0.07–0.10
 - Hard surfaces (concrete, pavement): 0.15–0.25

Material Properties

title: Assets: Import and Setup Surfaces
text:
e larger, more expansive tiling. Higher values produce tighter, denser tiling.

- Natural surfaces (grass, agriculture): 0.07–0.10
 - Hard surfaces (concrete, pavement): 0.15–0.25

Material Properties

- Base Color — An RGB colour multiplier applied on top of the Base Color Map. This field can be used without any textures assigned to create a flat colour surface.
- Metallic — is used as a multiplier to the values of metallic on the asset’s MaskMap (R), 0 is multiplying those imported values by 0 and 1 is multiplying by 1, that means if you set the slider to 1, you will see the metallic details imported from your maskmap. Values between 0 and 1 are supported.
- Smoothness — is used as a multiplier to the Glossiness values on the asset’s MaskMap (alpha channel), 0 is multiplying those imported values by 0 - cancelling them out, and 1 is multiplying by 1, that means if you set the slider to 1, you will see the Glossiness details imported from your maskmap. Values between 0 and 1 are supported.
- Normal Opa city — Controls the overall strength of the Normal Map. 1.0 is full strength. Reduce if the normal map appears too strong. 0 means the normal map will be invisible and the normals of the terrain below will show through the surface.
- Metallic Opa city - Controls the overall strength of the Metallic Map. 1.0 is full strength. Reduce if the metallic map appears too strong. 0 means the metallic map will be invisible and the normals of the terrain below will show through the surface.
- Normal Alpha Source - Controls the strength of the NormalMask data on the B Channel of MaskMap. 1.0 uses the masking information, 0 ignores the mask and overrides the mask to full white.
- Metallic Alpha Source - Controls the strength of the MetallicMask data on the G Channel of MaskMap. 1.0 uses the masking information, 0 ignores the mask and overrides the mask to full white.

title: Assets: Import and Setup Surfaces
text:
white.
- Metallic Alpha Source - Controls the strength of the MetallicMask data on the G Channel of MaskMap. 1.0 uses the masking information, 0 ignores the mask and overrides the mask to full white.

Edge Blending These settings control how the surface fades into the surrounding terrain at its boundary.

- Edge Fade Range — Two values defining the start point and width of the fade zone at the surface boundary.

- Hard surfaces: 0.55 / 0.45
 - Natural surfaces: 0.60 / 0.40

- Edge Noise — Adds irregularity to the fade boundary, preventing it from appearing as a clean stencil cut.

- Hard surfaces (crisp edge): 0.1 / 0.2
 - Natural surfaces (ragged edge): 0.5 / 0.7

- Edge Normal — Softens how strongly the Normal Map reads near the boundary, preventing bump detail from cutting off abruptly at the edge.

- Suggested range: 0.1–0.3

### Optional: UI Component

The following settings must be configured for your surface to appear in the player's UI:

- Icon — Path to an SVG icon or PNG representing your surface in the toolbar.
- Group — The menu your surface will appear in.
- Priority — The sort order of your surface within that panel. Lower numbers appear earlier.

### Optional: Spawnable Area

Set the placeholder surface you would like your custom surface to be used in the pool of that placeholders random spawning options, add a Spawnable Area component to your prefab.

title: Assets: Import and Setup Surfaces
text:
onal: Spawnable Area

Set the placeholder surface you would like your custom surface to be used in the pool of that placeholders random spawning options, add a Spawnable Area component to your prefab.

- Placeholders — One or more placeholder surfaces.
- Probability (0–100) — The chance that your surface is used when the placeholder surface is propped. Lower values make spawning probabilistic.
Note: The Agriculture Surface 01 in the base game uses a Probability of 100 as a reference example.

### Optional: Service Object

Associates the surface with a city service. The single dropdown is a reference to a ServicePrefab — one of the game's defined city services (Garbage, Parks, Industry, etc.). Adding this component signals to the game's simulation that placed instances of this surface contribute to or belong to that service.

This is used by the functional surfaces in the base game — Agriculture and Forestry surfaces are linked to the Industry service. A purely decorative surface would not need this component, or use only the Landscaping service if it is visible in the game's Landscaping menu.

### Optional: Unlockable

Controls when the surface becomes available to the player. The unlock check logic evaluates three things:

title: Assets: Import and Setup Surfaces
text:
andscaping service if it is visible in the game's Landscaping menu.

### Optional: Unlockable

Controls when the surface becomes available to the player. The unlock check logic evaluates three things:

- Require All — A list of prefabs that must ALL be unlocked before this surface becomes available. Every entry in this list is a hard requirement. If any one of them is still locked, the surface stays locked.
- Require Any — A list of prefabs where at least ONE being unlocked is sufficient to satisfy this condition. If you populate this list, the surface stays locked until the player has unlocked at least one entry from it.
- Ignore Dependencies — By default this is unchecked, meaning the game automatically scans the prefab's own referenced assets (its dependencies) and adds any that are themselves unlockable as additional Require All conditions. For example, if your surface references a Service Object linked to an unlockable service, the surface would automatically inherit that service's unlock requirement. Checking Ignore Dependencies disables that automatic inheritance — only the entries you've explicitly put in Require All and Require Any are used.
For a custom surface that should always be available to the player from the start, you can either leave the Unlockable component off entirely, or add it with both lists empty and Ignore Dependencies checked.

title: Assets: Import and Setup Surfaces
text:
ustom surface that should always be available to the player from the start, you can either leave the Unlockable component off entirely, or add it with both lists empty and Ignore Dependencies checked.

Remember to save your prefab frequently so you don't lost progress! Return to: Asset Creation GuideSee also: Asset Pipeline: Surfaces

# Assets: Importing

Source: https://cs2.paradoxwikis.com/Assets:_Importing

Verified by Paradox Interactive
This article has been verified by Paradox Interactive for version 1.5.2 f1 of the game.

## Preparation

In order to use the Asset Importer, first you need to set your import directories - Project Root and Asset Folder(s).

#### Project Root Directory

Project Root Directory is a central location where all of your ready-to-import custom asset files are located.

- We recommend using the name ‘ProjectFiles’ for the Root Directory.
- You can create this folder in any location on your PC, it is recommended to create it on a hard drive where you have sufficient space to store large files.

#### Asset Folder Directory

Meshes and textures that need to be imported should be placed inside an Asset Folder, which in turn is located in the Project Root.

- It is required to keep your Project Root directory clean and use ‘Asset Folder’ sub folders for each custom asset.
- The folder structure per asset or multiple assets depends on how you are importing custom assets - as some types of assets will require additional files, or less files due toTexture Sharing, or different import settings.
- Folder names should match the name and naming conventions of the main mesh within it. The name is used as the imported asset’s prefab name.

title: Assets: Importing Decals
text:
# Assets: Importing Decals

Source: https://cs2.paradoxwikis.com/Assets:_Importing_Decals

Paradox logo black square Verified by Paradox Interactive
This article has been verified by Paradox Interactive for version 1.5.2 f1 of the game.

## Import Settings

### Required Settings

AssetCreation ExampleDecalImport

Import Settings determine certain functions that need to be assigned during the import process. These values are generally automatically populated from the asset’s settings.json file within its Asset Folder. While it is good to check that information was correctly taken from the settings file, remember that texture sharing details are not shown in this section.

Your `settings.json` should be included in the Asset Folder with the following configuration:

```
{
 "SurfacePostProcessor": {
 "materialTemplate": "DefaultDecal"
 }
}

```

This tells the importer to apply the decal shader to your asset.

An alternative method can be done in the editor itself:

- In the Asset Importer panel expand the “Import Settings” container.
- In the “Add Settings” drop down select “SurfacePostProcessor”
- Under the new dropdown “Add Component” select “materialTemplate”
- In the text box next to “materialTemplate” type exactly “DefaultDecal”
- Save Settings, which writes the settings information to your asset’s `settings.json`.

### Optional Settings

title: Assets: Importing Decals

# Assets: Package, Share and Upload

Source: https://cs2.paradoxwikis.com/Assets:_Package%2C_Share_and_Upload

Verified by Paradox Interactive
This article has been verified by Paradox Interactive for version 1.5.2 f1 of the game.

## Build Package Panel

The Build Package Panel displays all of the selected asset’s dependencies and any dependent assets (assets that use the one you’ve selected). Assuming you have followed best practices for naming conventions, it will be clear which dependencies are assigned to each asset.
Each listed item is a different prefab or dependency used by your selected asset, from the respective locations in`ImportedData`. The Build Package Panel displays filenames instead of the internal prefab name, and as discussed inAssets: Importing - Imported Files, most local filenames will show some form of ID string added as a suffix to the original name.
Appended, in brackets, to the filenames after the ID string is the asset type. Some common asset types are:

title: Assets: Package, Share and Upload
text:
ost local filenames will show some form of ID string added as a suffix to the original name.
Appended, in brackets, to the filenames after the ID string is the asset type. Some common asset types are:

- `(PrefabAsset)`- usually the asset itself, render prefabs or another basic prefab.
- `(GeometryAsset)`- data for geometry of the imported mesh.
- `(TextureAsset)`- data for the imported textures.
- `(SurfaceAsset)`- data referencing the imported geometry and textures.
- `(LocaleAsset)`- localisation files for in-game texts.
- `(ImageAsset)`- usually thumbnail asset, unlock pop up images or other imported/available image files.

The initial list is automatically populated by the editor, and should have picked all related files for your asset.
Widen the Build Package Panel if it helps to see the full length of the filenames listed.

###

Picking Files to Package

- Packaging a Single Asset

Assets BuildPackageAssets dependency

- For a single asset, check that all listed items are directly related to your chosen asset, including any local dependencies and their own dependencies. Be careful to check for any other local assets you don’t wish to include.

- If you see other, unwanted assets, investigate how they are being used by your asset, and remove them.

title: Assets: Package, Share and Upload
text:
ir own dependencies. Be careful to check for any other local assets you don’t wish to include.

- If you see other, unwanted assets, investigate how they are being used by your asset, and remove them.

- To double check that all related files and prefabs have been correctly picked for packaging, open theAdd Assetdropdown and search for the common part of the filename shared by your asset’s imported files (from your naming convention).

- If the search returns items, review them and select any that are directly related to the asset. Continue selecting related files until no more appear in the search or you are satisfied that all necessary items have been included.
 - If the search lists no results, then you can be sure all related files have been picked for packaging.

- Packaging Multiple Assets

Assets BuildPackageAssets nodependency

title: Assets: Package, Share and Upload
text:
items have been included.
 - If the search lists no results, then you can be sure all related files have been picked for packaging.

- Packaging Multiple Assets

Assets BuildPackageAssets nodependency

- Use the previous steps underPackaging a Single Assetto check your initial asset has correctly picked all related files for packaging.
 - When you wish to add another asset to the Build Package list, open theAdd Assetdropdown and search for the filename of the new asset.
 - The best practice is to find the`(PrefabAsset)`for the new asset - making sure you aren’t selecting the`(PrefabAsset)`for the asset’s meshes. By selecting the correct file, all of the new asset’s dependencies, including the mesh`(PrefabAsset)`s should be automatically picked.
 - Check the picked items list again, following the same methodology for choosing single assets.
 - Repeat this process until you have selected all the assets you wish to upload to the same mod listing.

### Mod and DLC Dependencies

Assets BuildPackageContent

- If your asset is using subscribed assets, the game will automatically add the mod listing that contains the subscribed asset underMod Dependencies.
- If your asset is using items from a game pack, whether a Creator Pack or Expansion, the packs will be listed underDLC Dependencies.

title: Assets: Package, Share and Upload
text:
listing that contains the subscribed asset underMod Dependencies.
- If your asset is using items from a game pack, whether a Creator Pack or Expansion, the packs will be listed underDLC Dependencies.

If you’ve confirmed all your desired assets, the correct related dependencies and the Mod and DLC Dependencies are set, you can click theBuild Package. Building the package may take some time depending on the number and type of assets to be included. The game may appear to be unresponsive during this process. Please wait until the build finishes. Once your package is built, the Build Package Panel will automatically close and open the Upload Asset Panel.

## Upload Asset Panel

Assets Sharing

Upload Asset panel provides options that help you with publishing and creating the information for your Paradox Mods mod details page.

#### Asset Thumbnail

The large image at the top of the panel can be clicked on to open the image select tool. This allows you to browse any supported images that are located in your`C:\Users\<Username>\AppData\LocalLow\Colossal Order\Cities Skylines II\`directory.

- The image you select will be used as the thumbnail on Paradox Mods.
- Recommended to be minimum: 512x512 pixels
- Recommended to be maximum: 1024x1024 pixels
- Recommended format to be`.jpg`.
- The maximum size of the thumbnail is 2MB.
- Thumbnails are encouraged to be 1:1 ratio.

title: Assets: Package, Share and Upload
text:
d to be minimum: 512x512 pixels
- Recommended to be maximum: 1024x1024 pixels
- Recommended format to be`.jpg`.
- The maximum size of the thumbnail is 2MB.
- Thumbnails are encouraged to be 1:1 ratio.

- Any non-square image used will be cropped to size to fit a 1:1 preview.

Note: if any of the packaged files had DLC prerequisites the DLCs are listed below the thumbnail image.

#### Asset Upload Texts

- Asset Name

- This will be the name of your mod page on Paradox Mods.
 - Asset name has a maximum of60characters.
 - While you can be creative with your asset names, it is encouraged to keep the name of your mod upload concise and compact.

- Update previously shared assetcheckbox allows extra steps if you are uploading a new version on an asset previously shared to Paradox Mods.
See:Updating a Previously Shared Assetfor a more detailed approach.
- Short Description

- Descriptive text that is shown when users browse Paradox Mods via in-game browser.
 - Appears as the preview text on the rich embed.
 - Short Description can be no more than 200 characters long.

- Long Description

- The main descriptive text of your asset.
 - The Long Description can be up to 20,000 characters long. We encourage you to be conservative with your text.

#### Optional Links

- Forum Link

title: Assets: Package, Share and Upload
text:
cription

- The main descriptive text of your asset.
 - The Long Description can be up to 20,000 characters long. We encourage you to be conservative with your text.

#### Optional Links

- Forum Link

- Create a post in theUser Modssub-forum on the Paradox Forums to allow players to interact with you, and leave feedback in the comments. They appear under your mod’s description in Paradox Mods.
 - If you use the same forum link for all your assets, the comments will also be shared across any asset that is referring to the forum link.

- External Links

- Paradox Mods pages support a variety of external links, including social media, donation and GitHub. You can add multiple external links as you like.

#### Additional Parameters

- Screenshots

- You can add up to 10 screenshots.
 - Recommended format to be`.jpg`.
 - The maximum size of an individual screenshot is 2MB.

- Additional Tags

title: Assets: Package, Share and Upload

#### Additional Parameters

- Screenshots

- You can add up to 10 screenshots.
 - Recommended format to be`.jpg`.
 - The maximum size of an individual screenshot is 2MB.

- Additional Tags

- The editor will attempt to assign the tags it considers apply to your asset, or collection of assets up to a total of 10.
 - The beta implementation of theadd tagdropdown is a simple, unordered and unstructured list.
 - We recommend visitingMy uploaded modson Paradox Mods web, editing your mod page once it’s uploaded, and using thechoose tagsdropdown under theNametab. This list will be structured to reflect the layout in the side panel of Paradox Mods.
 - You can add up to 10 tags to your mod page. Tags appear on the right side panel of the platform and allows users to filter content based on their search preferences. Use relevant tags to keep browsing clear and accurate for other players.

- Additional Assets:- Lists the items that are packaged ready to be shared. This list can no longer be edited at this point.
- Mod Dependencies:- Automatically filled based on packaged assets’ dependencies of subscribed mods.
- Visibility Settings:- Set the visibility of your asset mod. Any user can have:

- Publicis the default setting.

- Keep intermediate files- allows you to keep a backup of the packaged`.cok`and source, unpackaged files used in this upload.

title: Assets: Package, Share and Upload
text:
ty of your asset mod. Any user can have:

- Publicis the default setting.

- Keep intermediate files- allows you to keep a backup of the packaged`.cok`and source, unpackaged files used in this upload.

- The backup will be kept in`C:\Users\yourname\AppData\LocalLow\Colossal Order\Cities Skylines II\.packages`

- Unpackaged backup files located here will not be loaded by the game.
 - Packaged backup`.cok`files located here will be loaded by the game as ‘read-only’ - they will only appear in thePackagescontainer in the Workspace

### Submit and beyond

Once everything is set, you can clicksubmitand your package will be uploaded to Paradox Mods. Submitting the asset completes your operations in the editor, you can safely close the game.

It is recommended to remove all of the local files used in your upload that are located in`C:\Users\yourname\AppData\LocalLow\Colossal Order\Cities Skylines II\ImportedData`and the sub folders it contains. This is to prevent any collisions with your upload if you choose to subscribe to it.
In general it is best to move themoutsideof the`C:\Users\yourname\AppData\LocalLow\Colossal Order\Cities Skylines II\`directory.

# Method 2:Package, Test Locally, then Share

title: Assets: Package, Share and Upload
text:
hoose to subscribe to it.
In general it is best to move themoutsideof the`C:\Users\yourname\AppData\LocalLow\Colossal Order\Cities Skylines II\`directory.

# Method 2:Package, Test Locally, then Share

This method is more complex, for advanced creators. It allows a creator to separate the process of packaging and sharing. This would mean you can package an asset, test out the resulting`.cok`file locally before uploading it. A lot of the processes will be very similar toMethod 1and will be referred to when necessary. Let’s begin.

Assets PackageFromWorkspace

- Either place your asset in the editor world space or select your prefab from the asset browser so it appears in the workspace.
- Next, locate the asset you want to package. If you are packaging multiple assets to the same`.cok`file, selecting any one of them is fine.
- Click the context menu (⋯) beside the asset name and choosePackage.
This will open the Build Package Panel on the right.
Note: assets have to be saved before they can be packaged. If you haven’t saved your asset you will be prompted to before the Build Package Panel is shown.

## Package…

This step is the same as inMethod 1. Refer to the instructions and information found in theBuild Package Panelsection.

###

Build Package Panel

####

Picking Files to Package

####

Mod and DLC Dependencies

title: Assets: Package, Share and Upload

p is the same as inMethod 1. Refer to the instructions and information found in theBuild Package Panelsection.

###

Build Package Panel

####

Picking Files to Package

####

Mod and DLC Dependencies

The key difference in the last step of that section, when you clickBuild Package, once the build finishes the panel will not change to the Upload Asset panel.

Instead, you will see at the top of the workspace a new container namedPackages. This will list any`.cok`files that are located in the`C:\Users\yourname\AppData\LocalLow\Colossal Order\Cities Skylines II\.packages`, where built packages are automatically saved to. These packages are only ‘read-only’ so you can’t test them locally just yet as they won’t properly load into the game.

Packages in this container are ready to be shared, simply by clicking the context menu (⋯) beside the package’s name and chooseShare.
This will open the Upload Asset Panel on the right, and allow you to submit to Paradox Mods.

But we’re not there yet. If you want totestthe package locally continue reading.

## ...Test Locally…

title: Assets: Package, Share and Upload
text:
are.
This will open the Upload Asset Panel on the right, and allow you to submit to Paradox Mods.

But we’re not there yet. If you want totestthe package locally continue reading.

## ...Test Locally…

This is where things can get complex fast. We need to move around some local files, and to do that the game needs to be closed.
Before we continue, make sure you’ve read and understood howImported Filesare structured. You must be very careful to remember and be able to recreate the structure of the files created during the import process.

### File Preparations for Testing

- Moving the Imported Files

- First, navigate to`C:\Users\yourname\AppData\LocalLow\Colossal Order\Cities Skylines II\ImportedData`and locate where the majority of your imported files are.
 - Carefully identify the files that were used during the packaging of your`.cok`.
 - Move all these files, whether they are located loosely in the import folder or a folder containing the`.prefab`files, remember where they were located as we will eventually put them back, if any updates or fixes are required.

- Relocating the Packaged File

title: Assets: Package, Share and Upload
text:
the import folder or a folder containing the`.prefab`files, remember where they were located as we will eventually put them back, if any updates or fixes are required.

- Relocating the Packaged File

- Navigate to`C:\Users\yourname\AppData\LocalLow\Colossal Order\Cities Skylines II\.packages`and identify the file you recently packaged. Similar to imported files, the package will also have an ID string appended to the filename.
 - Temporarily relocate the correct`.cok`file and its matching`.cid`file to`C:\Users\yourname\AppData\LocalLow\Colossal Order\Cities Skylines II\`folder.

Here’s what we’ve done:

- We moved the local individual and unpackaged files out of the game’s`AppData`directory so that it won’t be loaded, and can’t create a collision with its packaged counterpart.
- We temporarily moved the packaged file out of the`.packages`folder where it only loads as ‘read-only’ and into the main`Cities: Skylines II`folder so the game can load it fully.

Now the game can be launched and your packaged asset will be loaded so you can test it.

## ...thenShare

Now that you have tested your packaged asset, and you’re ready to share it to Paradox Mods, we need to reverse the last step we took when moving files around.

- Relocating the Packaged File Back

title: Assets: Package, Share and Upload
text:
are

Now that you have tested your packaged asset, and you’re ready to share it to Paradox Mods, we need to reverse the last step we took when moving files around.

- Relocating the Packaged File Back

- Close your game.
 - Find your packaged`.cok`file and its matching`.cid`in the`Cities: Skylines II`folder where we temporarily placed it.
 - Move both files back to the`C:\Users\yourname\AppData\LocalLow\Colossal Order\Cities Skylines II\.packages`folder.
 - Relaunch the game.

- Sharing the Tested Package

- Packages are found in the container at the top of the Workspace.
 - In this container packages are ready to be shared, simply by click the context menu (⋯) beside the package’s name and chooseShare.
This will open the Upload Asset Panel on the right.

#### Upload Asset Panel

Follow the steps in Method 1 on using the Upload Asset Panel, and share your asset to Paradox Mods. Once submitted, you can optionally remove or delete the package from the`.packages`folder depending on your needs.

# Updating a Previously Shared Asset

You can update an already uploaded Paradox Mods asset. It is recommended to be subscribed to the asset mod you’re looking to update butnothave it assigned to an active playset.

Having the asset subscribed and downloaded in your Paradox Mods is enough to capture any screenshots you may on the mod page. This will be important in the following steps.

title: Assets: Package, Share and Upload
text:
gned to an active playset.

Having the asset subscribed and downloaded in your Paradox Mods is enough to capture any screenshots you may on the mod page. This will be important in the following steps.

It’s recommended to not have more than one copy of the asset, packaged or unpackaged, loaded into the game when attempting to update the previously shared asset. Only have one instance of the asset. That could either be a set of local Imported Dataora newly packaged`.cok`file ready to share.

Follow either Method 1 or Method 2 until you reach the Upload Asset Panel. Then before making any changes to the panel:

- Check theUpdate Previously Shared Assetbox, located under asset name. Enabling this will add three extra parameters.
- Existing Assetlists all the assets you published to PDX Mods. You can click the asset you wish to update from the list. Once it's uploaded, the new package will override the existing one on the website.

- When you select an existing asset from Paradox Mods, the game will pull all data from the latest version, autofilling out the rest of the Upload Asset Panel.

Note:Screenshots will be pulled only if your previously shared mod is subscribed and downloaded into the Paradox Mods directory.

title: Assets: Package, Share and Upload
text:
he latest version, autofilling out the rest of the Upload Asset Panel.

Note:Screenshots will be pulled only if your previously shared mod is subscribed and downloaded into the Paradox Mods directory.

- Version number is a positive whole number(1,2,3,..). - check which version number your most recent iteration of the asset mod is on Paradox Mods, you will need to change the version number to be the next whole number.

- For example: if your asset mod’s latest version was 4, then you need to change the version number to 5.

- Changelog is a descriptive text used to list what previous changes were made with the updated version of the asset. It is good to be consistent with changelogs and mention if there’s any important change, since players can switch to older versions of mods..

# Obsolete Identifiers

Assets PackageObsoleteIdentifiers

Obsolete Identifiers is a component that is occasionally assigned to assets when their name changes, or when a reference needs to be maintained so that older saves can match a new asset name to its previous version.

During the packaging process, the game automatically adds this component and fills in the correct identifying information. This typically includes the existing internal name, the asset’s Prefab Component type, and a serialisation hash.

title: Assets: Package, Share and Upload
text:
ame automatically adds this component and fills in the correct identifying information. This typically includes the existing internal name, the asset’s Prefab Component type, and a serialisation hash.

If you update your asset but all naming conventions remain the same, the Obsolete Identifier information will remain unchanged and will not cause any issues.

If you do change the name of any of your files, once they are imported into the editor you will need to manually add the Obsolete Identifier component and assign it the same identifying information used by the older versions. This ensures that when you update the asset mod listing, any save that used the previous version can recognise the new one because it carries the correct identifying data.

title: Assets: Setting Up Color Variations
text:
# Assets: Setting Up Color Variations

Source: https://cs2.paradoxwikis.com/Assets:_Setting_Up_Color_Variations

Paradox logo black square Verified by Paradox Interactive
This article has been verified by Paradox Interactive for version 1.5.2 f1 of the game.

title: Assets: Setting Up Decals
text:
# Assets: Setting Up Decals

Source: https://cs2.paradoxwikis.com/Assets:_Setting_Up_Decals

Paradox logo black square Verified by Paradox Interactive
This article has been verified by Paradox Interactive for version 1.5.2 f1 of the game.

## Finding Your Mesh Render Prefab

Decal properties are controlled on the render prefab in the workspace once you have imported your decal. See Asset Pipeline: Decals for specific details on how to set up the Decal meshes and textures.

To be able to set up our Decals and edit their properties, we need to find the imported mesh Render Prefab.

There are 2 ways to find your mesh’s Render Prefab:

1. Place your custom asset, which has the mesh assigned to it, in the editor worldspace.

1. Find this asset in the “Object” section of the workspace panel.
 2. Expand the asset dropdown arrow to reveal the assigned mesh Render Prefabs.
 3. Select the Render Prefab you wish to set up emissives on to inspect it.

2. Open the Asset Browser.

1. Select the category on the left panel “Render Prefabs”.
 2. Search for the name of your mesh, this is usually assigned to the Render Prefab if you have followed the import naming conventions correctly.
 3. Select your mesh Render Prefab, which will place it into the “Prefabs” section of the workspace panel, allowing you to inspect it.

## Decal Properties Component

title: Assets: Setting Up Decals
text:
port naming conventions correctly.
 3. Select your mesh Render Prefab, which will place it into the “Prefabs” section of the workspace panel, allowing you to inspect it.

## Decal Properties Component

Now you have your mesh’s Render Prefab selected, we should be able to see the “Decal Properties” component which is usually automatically added whenever you follow the Import Settings from Assets: Importing Decals.

If you don’t see the component, we can use the “add component” button to set the required component.

- Click that button which opens a list of available components to add to a Render Prefab.
- search for “Decal Properties” and select it to apply it.

### Texture Area

The Min and Max’s ‘x’ and ‘y’ values are determining the position of the decal’s projection on the UV mapped surface. This is usually automatically assigned on import, but can be manually overridden if necessary.

### Render Priority

This value sets the stacking priority when rendering the decal. If other decal assets are stacked on top of one another, the priority sets which one is rendered on top, most visible to the camera.

### Layer Mask

This is a multi-select dropdown that sets which types of game surfaces will the decal project onto. By default Terrain, Roads and Buildings are selected.

### Enable Infoview Color

title: Assets: Setting Up Decals
text:
a.

### Layer Mask

This is a multi-select dropdown that sets which types of game surfaces will the decal project onto. By default Terrain, Roads and Buildings are selected.

### Enable Infoview Color

This checkbox allows the decal to be rendered with the color of the appropriate infoview if the asset has additional values set to it that correspond with a specific infoview. Usually this would remain unchecked.

# Decal Asset Properties

A decal is a static object prefab and does not require additional properties to function if it’s intended for decoration only.

You may add UI Object components and other components depending on your needs. Investigate existing decal prefabs to use them as examples for how to set up the decal how you desire.

For decals that would project onto a wall, or otherwise not downward you should rotate the mesh element’s ‘x’ rotation value in the meshes dropdown list on the asset. Return to: ModdingSee also: Common Asset Editor Principles for information on adding other asset components to your static object prefab.

title: Assets: Texture Sharing
text:
# Assets: Texture Sharing

Source: https://cs2.paradoxwikis.com/Assets:_Texture_Sharing

Verified by Paradox Interactive
This article has been verified by Paradox Interactive for version 1.5.2 f1 of the game.

## Texture Sharing

Texture sharing allows you to re-use the same texture set across multiple assets, without duplicating any file. While it is great to use to keep asset upload sizes lower, it is worth keeping the following limitations and best practices in mind:

- A whole set of textures, (BaseColor, ControlMask, etc…) should be shared, not individual textures.
- Sharing textures is most optimised for the game when the assets sharing textures are shown together in the camera’s render view at the same time. That way:

- A sub building or extension can share textures with the main asset as they are usually placed together.
 - Props on a building it shares textures with are rendered in the same view.

- LOD1 should share textures with the main mesh, as the textures don’t need to change based on zoom levels. Instead virtual texturing can optimize between the main mesh and LOD1.

For texture sharing, you need to create a .json configuration file named`settings.json`. Most text editors should be able to do this, you can simply create a .txt file and change the file extension.

title: Assets: Texture Sharing
text:
For texture sharing, you need to create a .json configuration file named`settings.json`. Most text editors should be able to do this, you can simply create a .txt file and change the file extension.

The .json file should be located inside the Asset Folder of the asset that requires the texture sharing. The overall layout of texture file path entries follows this convention, where`"SharedTo_Texture.png"`is the texture you wish to create by using the`"SharedFrom_Texture.png"`as a reference:

```
"SharedTo_Texture.png": "SharedFrom_Texture.png"

```

The path for`"SharedFrom_Texture.png"`should always been using Relative Pathing, not absolute pathing.

## settings.json Examples

### Example 1

LOD1 Shares the Main Textures

If you are using texture sharing for LOD1 meshes, both main mesh, its textures and LOD1 meshes are in the same Asset Folder. A`settings.json`should also be included in the Asset Folder with the following configuration:

```
{
 "sharedAssets": {
 "ExampleAsset_LOD1_BaseColor.png": "ExampleAsset_BaseColor.png",
 "ExampleAsset_LOD1_ControlMask.png": "ExampleAsset_ControlMask.png",
 "ExampleAsset_LOD1_Emissive.png": "ExampleAsset_Emissive.png",
 "ExampleAsset_LOD1_MaskMap.png": "ExampleAsset_MaskMap.png",
 "ExampleAsset_LOD1_Normal.png": "ExampleAsset_Normal.png"
 }
}

```

### Example 2

Main Mesh, and LOD1 both Share Textures from Another Mesh

title: Assets: Texture Sharing
text:
ampleAsset_LOD1_MaskMap.png": "ExampleAsset_MaskMap.png",
 "ExampleAsset_LOD1_Normal.png": "ExampleAsset_Normal.png"
 }
}

```

### Example 2

Main Mesh, and LOD1 both Share Textures from Another Mesh

When wishing to import “ExampleAsset01” and using the textures from “ExampleAsset02” use the following structure.

Make sure the textures in the`"SharedFrom_Texture.png"`position (in this case the textures from “ExampleAsset02”) are using Relative Pathing to find the correct path.

The`settings.json`should be placed inside the Asset Folder for “ExampleAsset01”.

title: Assets: Texture Sharing
text:

The`settings.json`should be placed inside the Asset Folder for “ExampleAsset01”.
```

 "ExampleAsset01_BaseColor.png": "../ExampleAsset02/ExampleAsset02_BaseColor.png",

```
### Example 3

When wishing to import “ExampleAsset01” and using the textures from “ExampleAsset02” for the main mesh, the LOD1 mesh and have LOD2 mesh use the textures from LOD2 of “ExampleAsset02”, use the following structure.
```

title: Assets: Texture Sharing
text:
LOD2_Emissive.png",

```
Note: Above, LOD2 of "ExampleAsset01" is sharing textures from the LOD2 of "ExampleAsset02" which would be in the same relative location as the other textures.

Based on these examples, you can create your own configuration files.

## Important Notes to Remember

- Texture sharing during the import process occurs per Asset Folder.
- Pathing should be relative.
- Batch import supports texture sharing.
- Batch import supports assets with shared textures located in different directories.
- Assets and meshes that share textures must be packaged in the same`.cok`file. You cannot share textures across different packages.
- You cannot share textures with any built-in assets.
- If you individually import assets that share textures, you may notice the interior window effect will go dark for the session. A restart fixes this issue.

When in the Asset Importer, on selecting your Asset Folder you will see a list of files that will be imported by the game. For a conventional asset, the list should contain all meshes, sub meshes, textures and setting files. If you are batch importing, you will have the same complete list but for all the assets that will be imported.

title: Assets: Texture Sharing
text:
tional asset, the list should contain all meshes, sub meshes, textures and setting files. If you are batch importing, you will have the same complete list but for all the assets that will be imported.

The list of`"SharedTo_Texture.png"`textures will be listed under settings.json file.

Verify that your textures are properly listed before proceeding to import.
```
